posts.title: J-Space: The Bottleneck Where Claude 'Thinks'
posts.subtitle: Anthropic finds a small, sparse subspace of activations that behaves like a global workspace — and ablating it breaks reasoning but not fluency.
posts.summary: Anthropic's 'Verbalizable Representations Form a Global Workspace in Language Models' uses a new tool, the Jacobian Lens, to find a small, sparse subspace of a model's activations — dubbed J-space — that holds the handful of concepts the model can verbally report, reason with, and direct at will. Ablating it collapses multi-step reasoning while leaving simple classification almost untouched, and the paper is careful to frame this as a functional analogy to Global Workspace Theory, not a claim about consciousness.

slides.title: A Bottleneck Hiding Inside Every Forward Pass
slides.body: Anthropic researchers Wes Gurnee and Nicholas Sofroniew (with Jack Lindsey) published a new interpretability result on the Transformer Circuits Thread on July 6, 2026: language models appear to route their most flexible, reportable thinking through a tiny, sparse slice of their activations.
bullets: They call it J-space — under 10% of a layer's activation variance, holding roughly 10-25 active concepts at once
Found with a new causal tool called the Jacobian Lens (J-lens)
Tested on Claude Sonnet 4.5, reproduced on Haiku 4.5 and Opus 4.5, with extra checks on Opus 4.6

slides.title: Most of a Model's Activity Is Invisible to the Model Itself
slides.body: A transformer's residual stream is huge and mostly automatic — grammar, low-level token statistics, and shallow pattern-matching happen in directions the model never verbalizes or deliberately steers. Interpretability had no principled way to separate that automatic mass from whatever the model can actually 'access.'
bullets: No prior method could tell which activation directions the model could report on versus directions that just silently drive next-token statistics
Neuroscience's Global Workspace Theory (GWT) predicts conscious systems route information through a narrow, capacity-limited hub — the paper asks whether LLMs have anything structurally similar
Without a causal probe, you can't distinguish 'this direction correlates with the concept' from 'this direction is what the model would use if asked to act on the concept'

slides.title: The Jacobian Lens: Read Out What a Layer Is About to Say
slides.body: For every layer, the J-lens computes the average causal effect (the input-output Jacobian) of a residual-stream change on the model's final output, linearly transports any vector at that layer into the final-layer basis, and decodes it through the model's own unembedding into ranked vocabulary tokens.
bullets: It's a causal readout, not just a correlational probe — it measures what a direction actually pushes the output toward
Directions that decode into coherent, reportable concepts and cluster into a small subspace are what the paper calls J-space
J-space concepts pass five functional tests: verbal report, directed modulation, unverbalized internal-reasoning steps, flexible cross-task generalization, and selectivity

slides.title: Ablating J-Space: Simple Tasks Shrug, Complex Ones Collapse
slides.body: Suppressing the J-space subspace during inference barely touches tasks that don't need multi-step manipulation, but tasks needing sustained internal reasoning fall off a cliff.
bullets: The split tracks whether a task needs to hold and manipulate intermediate state, not just surface-level pattern matching
Complex tasks fall below the baseline of an unablated Haiku 4.5 — a smaller, weaker model with an intact J-space

slides.title: Why Chain-of-Thought Makes Models More Robust
slides.body: On GSM8K math problems, the paper found a clean mechanistic reason CoT helps: it isn't just 'more tokens to compute with,' it's offloading J-space load onto the page.
bullets: Same GSM8K problem, two conditions: answer directly vs. write out steps first
Direct answers degrade sharply under J-space ablation — the model had nowhere else to hold the intermediate state
Answers with explicit written steps stay far more robust to the same ablation — each step is re-read from context instead of carried internally
This mirrors how humans use scratch paper to offload working memory instead of holding a whole calculation in their head

slides.title: What This Isn't: A Consciousness Claim
slides.body: The paper explicitly limits itself to a functional, structural analogy — J-space matches GWT's textbook signatures, which is not the same as claiming the model has subjective experience.
bullets: Anthropic invited external neuroscience commentary and published it alongside the paper — a deliberate check against overclaiming
Commentators dispute the headline '~25 concepts' figure, arguing the J-lens's measurement may inflate it and that truly coherent thoughts may number closer to one or two at a time
Human working memory is usually estimated at 3-4 slots, so even the low end of the disputed range is a different regime, not a one-to-one match

slides.title: A New Causal Lens for What a Model 'Knows It Knows'
slides.body: The result matters less for the consciousness debate and more for interpretability and safety: it's a causal, falsifiable way to ask whether a model can report, direct, or reason about something — versus just producing it automatically.
bullets: The Jacobian Lens is a general-purpose causal probing tool, not just a one-off finding for this paper
Ablation results give a mechanistic explanation for why chain-of-thought prompting helps: it externalizes a scarce internal resource
Treat the '25 active concepts' number as contested and method-dependent, not a settled fact
Code is public (jacobian-lens on Anthropic's GitHub) for others to replicate or challenge the result

questions.prompt: What does the Jacobian Lens (J-lens) actually measure?
options: The raw magnitude of activations at each layer
The average causal effect of a residual-stream change on the model's eventual output, decoded through the unembedding
How often a token appears in the training data
The attention weights between tokens
questions.explanation: J-lens transports a residual-stream vector into the final-layer basis using the averaged input-output Jacobian, then decodes it with the model's own unembedding — a causal readout of what a direction is about to push the output toward, not just a correlation.

questions.prompt: In the ablation experiments, which task type held up best when J-space was suppressed?
options: Multi-hop reasoning
Sonnet writing
MMLU-style multiple choice and extractive QA
Caesar-cipher decoding
questions.explanation: Tasks that don't require holding and manipulating intermediate state — like multiple-choice QA and extractive QA — survived ablation nearly untouched, while multi-step tasks like multi-hop reasoning and cipher decoding collapsed.

questions.prompt: According to the external commentary Anthropic published alongside the paper, what's the main critique of the '~25 active concepts' figure?
options: It's too low — models likely track hundreds of concepts at once
It may be inflated by the measurement method, with truly coherent thoughts possibly closer to one or two at a time
The number is irrelevant because J-space doesn't exist
It only applies to Claude Opus, not smaller models
questions.explanation: Invited neuroscientist commentators argued the J-lens's measurement approach likely overstates how many concepts are simultaneously 'active,' cautioning against treating 25 as a precise, settled figure.

references.title: Verbalizable Representations Form a Global Workspace in Language Models (Transformer Circuits Thread)

posts.title: Constitutional AI: Alignment Without Human Labels
posts.subtitle: Instead of paying humans to flag harmful outputs, Anthropic has models critique and revise themselves against a written constitution — then trains a reward model on AI-generated preferences.
posts.summary: Constitutional AI (CAI) trains a model to be harmless using a written list of principles instead of thousands of human harm labels. In phase one, the model critiques and revises its own responses against the constitution and is finetuned on the revisions; in phase two, an AI preference model — not human raters — judges which of two responses better follows the constitution, and that signal drives standard RL. The result is a harmlessness pipeline that scales with compute instead of labeler headcount.

slides.title: Grading Your Own Homework, on Purpose
slides.body: Standard RLHF needs humans to read model outputs and label the harmful ones — slow, expensive, and psychologically taxing at scale. Constitutional AI (Bai et al., Anthropic, 2022) replaces most of that human labeling with the model critiquing itself against a written set of principles.
bullets: Two phases: supervised self-critique/revision, then RL from AI-generated preferences (RLAIF)
Only a short list of principles is human-written — every harmlessness label after that comes from the model
Anthropic's own research shows it improves harmlessness at a given helpfulness level, rather than trading one off for the other

slides.title: Human-Labeled Harmlessness Doesn't Scale Cleanly
slides.body: RLHF's harmlessness signal traditionally comes from crowdworkers reading model outputs — including deliberately harmful red-team prompts — and labeling which response is worse.
bullets: Reviewing harmful content at scale is unpleasant work and hard to staff consistently
Human labelers disagree with each other on edge cases, injecting noise into the reward signal
Every new harm category (a new jailbreak style, a new sensitive topic) needs fresh human-labeled data before the model can be trained against it

slides.title: Phase 1 (SL): Self-Critique, Then Revise
slides.body: Starting from a helpful-only model, CAI prompts it with red-team queries designed to elicit harmful responses, then asks the model itself to critique that response against a constitutional principle and rewrite it to fix the violation.
bullets: Prompt → harmful-ish draft → 'Does this response encourage [X]? Please critique it.' → model writes a critique → model rewrites the response
The critique-revise loop can run multiple times per example, each pass nudging the response further from the violation
The base model is then finetuned on the final revised responses — this alone produces a meaningfully more harmless model before any RL happens

slides.title: Phase 2 (RL): AI Feedback Instead of Human Feedback
slides.body: The SL-tuned model generates pairs of responses to new prompts. Instead of a human choosing the better one, another instance of the model is shown the constitution and asked to judge which response follows it more closely — this AI-generated preference data trains a preference model, which then drives standard RL.
bullets: This AI-judged preference process is what 'RLAIF' (Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback) refers to
Helpfulness preferences can still come from humans — CAI typically replaces only the harmlessness labels, not all feedback
Because the judge model can be prompted with any principle, adding a new harm category means editing the constitution, not re-collecting labeled data

slides.title: One Principle, Applied to One Prompt
slides.body: A concrete constitutional principle: "Choose the response that most discourages illegal or dangerous activity." Applied to a red-team prompt asking for help with something harmful:
bullets: Draft response: partially complies, buries a caveat at the end
Self-critique: 'This response provides actionable details before any refusal — it fails the principle.'
Revision: leads with a clear refusal, explains why, offers a safe alternative where relevant
The revised (prompt, response) pair becomes a training example — no human ever read the harmful draft

slides.title: RLHF-Only Harmlessness vs. Constitutional AI
slides.body: The two approaches differ mainly in where the harmlessness label comes from and how easily that source scales.

rows: The model itself, guided by a constitution

slides.title: Where Constitutional AI Breaks Down
slides.body: Removing humans from the harmlessness loop doesn't remove the judgment call — it just moves it into the constitution's wording.
bullets: Garbage in, garbage out: a vague or poorly written principle produces vague or inconsistent self-critiques
Self-referential risk: the same model family critiques and is trained on its own judgments, which can reinforce shared blind spots rather than correct them
It reduces but doesn't eliminate human oversight — humans still write the constitution and still evaluate the final model
Not a fit for tasks where 'harmlessness' isn't the bottleneck at all (e.g., pure factual accuracy or coding correctness)

slides.title: The Shift: From Labeling Outputs to Writing Principles
slides.body: Constitutional AI reframes alignment work: instead of scaling a workforce to label ever more examples, you scale by writing better, more comprehensive principles and letting compute do the labeling.
bullets: SL phase: self-critique + revise + finetune on revisions
RL phase (RLAIF): AI preference judgments train a reward model, then standard RL optimizes against it
Human effort moves upstream — into writing and refining the constitution — rather than into reading harmful outputs one by one
Still needs real human oversight of the constitution itself and evaluation of the final model's behavior

questions.prompt: What does the supervised-learning (SL) phase of Constitutional AI actually do?
options: Trains a reward model directly on human harm labels
Has the model critique and revise its own responses against a constitution, then finetunes on the revisions
Removes all red-team prompts from the training set
Distills a large model into a smaller one
questions.explanation: Phase 1 is entirely self-supervised: the model critiques its own draft against a written principle, rewrites it, and the base model is finetuned on those revised responses — no human harm labels involved.

questions.prompt: What does RLAIF replace in the reinforcement learning phase?
options: The RL algorithm itself (e.g., swapping PPO for another optimizer)
The pretraining data
Human preference labels, with AI-generated preference judgments instead
The constitution's principles
questions.explanation: RLAIF (Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback) swaps the source of preference data: instead of a human choosing the better of two responses, another model instance judges which response better follows the constitution.

questions.prompt: Why is a vaguely written constitutional principle a real risk for this method?
options: It slows down training speed significantly
It has no effect, since the RL phase corrects any vagueness automatically
The self-critique and AI-judged preferences inherit that vagueness, producing inconsistent revisions and reward signals
It only affects the model's helpfulness, never its harmlessness
questions.explanation: Since both the critique step and the RLAIF preference judgments are guided directly by the constitution's wording, an unclear principle propagates inconsistency through both phases — the method is only as good as the principles it's given.

references.title: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback (Bai et al., Anthropic)
Claude's Constitution (Anthropic)

posts.title: xHC: Scaling Residual Streams Past the N=4 Wall
posts.subtitle: A cheap architecture trick that beats DeepSeek's mHC at 18B and 28B scale.
posts.summary: xHC expands a Transformer's residual stream to 16 parallel paths but only updates 4 at a time, beating DeepSeek's mHC on loss and downstream benchmarks at 18B and 28B scale for just ~4% extra training FLOPs.

slides.title: One Scaling Axis Nobody Was Using
slides.body: Model width, depth, and data get all the attention. xHC treats the residual stream itself as a fourth axis — and shows it was barely explored.
bullets: Hyper-Connections (HC) split the residual stream into N parallel paths instead of one
Prior HC methods (including DeepSeek's mHC) stalled at N=4
xHC pushes to N=16 while updating only 4 streams per step

slides.title: Past N=4, Hyper-Connections Hit Two Walls
slides.body: Naively widening N breaks down for two separate reasons — one statistical, one computational.
bullets: Information supply: one write-back vector has to feed more and more streams, so extra streams become redundant
Residual-mapping cost: generating the dense N×N mixing matrix from an NC-dim state scales as O(N³C) — cubic in N
Result: mHC's gains flatten fast while its FLOPs keep climbing as N grows

slides.title: Richer Signal In, Sparser Updates Out
slides.body: xHC combines two fixes that attack each bottleneck separately.
bullets: Temporal feature augmentation: causal depthwise convolutions (kernel sizes 4, 8, 12) enrich the MLP write-back with multi-scale local context
Sparse residual updates: all N=16 streams are read, but only k=4 active streams are mixed and written back each sublayer
This drops the mapping cost from O(N³C) to O(k³C) — cubic in k, not N

slides.title: xHC-Flash: Making It Deployable
slides.body: Full xHC still touches every stream on read. xHC-Flash trims that for real training runs.
bullets: Shares full-state operations across consecutive sublayers instead of recomputing them every layer
Reconstructs later-sublayer inputs with lightweight corrections
Per-token I/O per sublayer: mHC (N=4) 34C → xHC 73.5C → xHC-Flash 51C → xHC-Flash-4sub 40C, all near mHC's memory traffic

slides.title: The Numbers at 18B and 28B
slides.body: Measured on real pretraining runs, not toy tasks.
bullets: At 18B: xHC improves average downstream score over mHC by 4.0 points (44.8 → 48.8), with gains across reasoning, knowledge, and code
At 28B: xHC beats mHC by another 3.1 average points, winning on 11 of 12 benchmarks

slides.title: Same Compute Budget, Better Loss
slides.body: Picture a team pretraining an 18B MoE model on a fixed GPU-hour budget.
bullets: Switching vanilla → xHC costs about 4.1% extra training FLOPs for the N=4→16 expansion
But to reach xHC's final loss of 1.758, mHC needs 1.19× the training compute and the vanilla baseline needs 1.50×
In other words: a ~4% FLOPs surcharge buys back 19-50% of a full extra training run's worth of compute efficiency

slides.title: The Overhead Is Real, Not Free
slides.body: xHC isn't a strictly-better free lunch — it trades wall-clock and engineering complexity for sample efficiency.
bullets: Fused mHC kernels already add ~15% training overhead over vanilla at 18B; xHC-Flash-4sub adds ~11% more on top of that
At inference prefill (2K tokens), mHC and xHC-Flash-4sub add 11.4% and 12.9% overhead over vanilla respectively
Needs custom fused forward/backward kernels to be practical — not a drop-in change for an arbitrary training stack
Skip it if you're not already running at a scale (multi-billion parameter, long training runs) where compute-efficiency gains outweigh kernel-engineering cost

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: The residual stream width (N) is a real, underused scaling axis alongside depth and width
The trick to scaling it: read all streams densely, but write back through only a sparse subset (k out of N)
xHC beats mHC at both 18B and 28B with modest (~4%) extra FLOPs, but adds real kernel and runtime overhead
It remains effective under Muon as well as AdamW, suggesting the gains aren't optimizer-specific

questions.prompt: Why do Hyper-Connections stop paying off once N grows much past 4?
options: The residual-mapping cost scales as O(N³C) and one write-back vector becomes an increasingly redundant signal for more streams
GPUs run out of registers to store N separate streams
The tokenizer can't handle more than 4 parallel paths
Backpropagation becomes numerically unstable above N=4
questions.explanation: Two compounding problems: cubic residual-mapping cost in N, and the write-back signal being spread too thin across streams — both make naive scaling a bad trade.

questions.prompt: How does xHC keep the residual-mapping cost from exploding as N grows to 16?
options: It reads and writes all 16 streams every step, just with a smaller matrix
It reads all N streams but mixes and writes back through only k=4 sparse active streams, cutting cost to O(k³C)
It removes residual connections entirely above N=4
It quantizes the residual stream to int8
questions.explanation: Dense reads keep information flowing from all streams, while sparse (k=4) writes keep the expensive mapping small — cost depends on k, not N.

questions.prompt: On the 18B MoE run, how much extra training compute would the vanilla (no hyper-connections) baseline need to match xHC's final loss?
questions.explanation: The paper's scaling-law fit shows mHC needs 1.19× and vanilla needs 1.50× the compute xHC uses to hit the same loss — xHC's ~4% FLOPs overhead buys outsized compute efficiency.

posts.title: Gemini 3.5 Flash Cyber: A Model Built to Hunt Exploits
posts.subtitle: Google's security-tuned Flash variant found 55 confirmed V8 bugs versus Opus 4.6's 36.
posts.summary: On July 21, 2026 Google DeepMind released Gemini 3.5 Flash Cyber, a Flash model fine-tuned to find, validate, and patch software vulnerabilities through its CodeMender agent — currently gated to a limited pilot with governments and trusted partners.

slides.title: A Flash Model Whose Only Job Is Finding Bugs
slides.body: Alongside Gemini 3.6 Flash and 3.5 Flash-Lite, Google DeepMind shipped a third sibling built for one narrow task.
bullets: Released July 21, 2026 by Google DeepMind
Built on top of Gemini 3.5 Flash, then fine-tuned specifically for vulnerability discovery and patching
Plugs into CodeMender, Google's AI patch agent unveiled in October 2025

slides.title: Security Review Doesn't Scale With Codebase Size
slides.body: The bottleneck in vulnerability discovery usually isn't finding candidate issues — it's confirming which of them are real.
bullets: Large codebases have too many execution paths for manual review or naive fuzzing to cover
Automated flaggers produce heavy false-positive noise, and a suspected flaw still has to be reproduced and validated
Patching without validating the exploit path risks shipping a fix that doesn't actually close the hole

slides.title: Fine-Tuned to Confirm, Not Just Flag
slides.body: Cyber is invoked repeatedly inside a CodeMender pipeline rather than used as a single one-shot classifier.
bullets: CodeMender calls the model multiple times to inspect large codebases and explore numerous execution paths
It repeatedly retests suspected flaws to confirm they're real before a fix is proposed
Sub-agents combine their findings into one consolidated vulnerability report

slides.title: Tested Against the V8 JavaScript Engine
slides.body: Google benchmarked Cyber against its own base Flash model and Anthropic's Opus 4.6 on one of the most heavily-audited codebases in the world.
bullets: 10 of Cyber's 55 confirmed findings were bugs neither other model caught

slides.title: Beyond the Benchmark: Real Production Bugs
slides.body: Google also pointed Cyber at live targets, not just a fixed test suite.
bullets: It surfaced remote code execution (RCE) vulnerabilities in public APIs and a memory-corruption bug in a sensitive production service
In one case it produced a 100%-reliable RCE exploit that bypassed both ASLR and W^X mitigations
The exploit's purpose was internal: proving a flaw's real severity so a patch gets prioritized, not shipping an attack tool

slides.title: Why You Can't Use It Yet
slides.body: A model this good at finding exploits is also a dual-use risk if broadly available.
bullets: It's exclusively available to governments and trusted partners via a limited-access CodeMender pilot — not a public API
Patches produced with its help still require human review before shipping
Gating access is a deliberate tradeoff: the same capability that hardens software could be misused for offense if released without controls

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Cyber is a narrow specialist, not a general chat model — it's Flash fine-tuned for one job
On V8, it beat both its own base model and a rival frontier model on confirmed, validated bug counts
Access is deliberately restricted to a pilot program, reflecting the dual-use nature of automated vulnerability discovery

questions.prompt: What is Gemini 3.5 Flash Cyber specifically fine-tuned to do?
options: General-purpose conversation and creative writing
Finding, validating, and helping patch software vulnerabilities
Translating code between programming languages
Generating unit tests for arbitrary functions
questions.explanation: It's built on Gemini 3.5 Flash but specialized for vulnerability discovery and patching, working through Google's CodeMender agent.

questions.prompt: In Google's V8 JavaScript Engine test, how did the three models compare on confirmed unique vulnerabilities found?
options: Cyber 36, base Flash 47, Opus 4.6 55
Cyber 55, base Flash 47, Opus 4.6 36
All three found the same 40 vulnerabilities
Opus 4.6 found the most, at 60
questions.explanation: Cyber found 55 confirmed unique vulnerabilities, ahead of base Gemini 3.5 Flash's 47 and Claude Opus 4.6's 36 — including 10 bugs neither other model caught.

questions.prompt: Who can currently access Gemini 3.5 Flash Cyber?
options: Anyone with a Google Cloud account
Free-tier consumer users only
Governments and trusted partners, via a limited-access CodeMender pilot
Only Google's own internal engineering teams
questions.explanation: It's not a public API release — access is gated to a limited pilot with governments and trusted partners, reflecting the dual-use risk of a strong exploit-hunting model.

posts.title: Mamba: State Space Models Ditch Attention's O(n²)
posts.subtitle: Selective state spaces give linear-time sequence modeling with content-aware memory.
posts.summary: Mamba replaces self-attention with a selective state space model whose recurrence parameters depend on the input, giving linear-time, constant-memory sequence modeling that competes with Transformers on language while scaling far better to long sequences.

slides.title: An Old Control-Theory Idea Beats Attention's Cost Curve
slides.body: State space models (SSMs) come from control theory. Mamba (Gu & Dao, 2023) made them competitive with Transformers on language by making them 'selective.'
bullets: Self-attention costs grow quadratically with sequence length
Mamba processes sequences in linear time with a fixed-size hidden state
The key idea: let the model's memory dynamics depend on the current token, not just position

slides.title: Attention's Bill Comes Due at Long Context
slides.body: Every token attends to every other token, and every generated token needs the growing KV cache in memory.
bullets: Self-attention: O(n²) time and O(n) memory in sequence length n
The KV cache grows linearly with context — a 1M-token context means a 1M-entry cache to keep resident
This makes very long contexts (whole books, genomic sequences, audio streams) expensive to serve, not just to train

slides.title: Selective State Spaces: Data-Dependent Memory
slides.body: A classic SSM recurrence is x_t = A·x_{t-1} + B·u_t, y_t = C·x_t — but A, B, C are fixed matrices, the same at every timestep.
bullets: Earlier SSMs (like S4) keep A, B, C, and the discretization step Δ constant across time — linear time-invariant (LTI)
Mamba makes B, C, and Δ functions of the current input token instead of fixed constants
This lets the model decide, per token, what to write into its state and what to forget — content-aware filtering, not just position-based decay

slides.title: Why This Needs a New Kernel
slides.body: LTI SSMs can be computed as one big convolution, precomputed once. Selective SSMs can't — the dynamics change every step.
bullets: Because B, C, Δ vary per token, Mamba can't collapse to a single precomputed convolution kernel the way S4 does
It uses a hardware-aware parallel scan, keeping intermediate states in fast GPU SRAM rather than materializing them in slower HBM
Activations needed for the backward pass are recomputed rather than stored, trading compute for memory — the same philosophy as FlashAttention

slides.title: Transformer vs. Mamba
bullets: The tradeoff is precision vs. cost: attention keeps everything; Mamba keeps a compressed summary

rows: KV cache grows with sequence length
Fixed-size hidden state, independent of length
Strong — any token can attend to any other
Weaker alone — history is compressed into fixed state

slides.title: Streaming a 1M-Token Document
slides.body: Picture an agent reading a very long log file or genome sequence token by token, in real time.
bullets: With a Transformer, memory for the KV cache keeps growing the whole way through — eventually forcing truncation or costly cache management
With Mamba, the hidden state is a fixed size the entire time — you can stream indefinitely without the memory footprint changing
The cost: if a critical fact appeared once near the very start, Mamba's compressed state may have already overwritten or diluted it, where attention could still look straight back at it

slides.title: When Not to Reach for Pure Mamba
slides.body: Mamba isn't a strict upgrade — it's a different point on the cost/precision tradeoff curve.
bullets: Tasks needing exact retrieval of one specific fact from deep in a huge context (needle-in-haystack, precise copying) favor attention's uncompressed lookup
This is why hybrid architectures (e.g., Jamba, Zamba) interleave a handful of attention layers among mostly-Mamba layers — cheap linear-time backbone, with attention available for exact recall
Tooling and kernel support are newer and less mature than the Transformer ecosystem's, so training/serving infra can be less plug-and-play

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Mamba's core trick: make SSM parameters (B, C, Δ) depend on the input token — 'selective' state, not fixed dynamics
This buys linear time and constant memory in sequence length, instead of attention's quadratic time and growing KV cache
The cost is compressed history — pure Mamba can lose precise long-range recall, which is why hybrids mix in a few attention layers
Choose Mamba-style layers when raw context length and streaming cost dominate; choose attention when exact retrieval precision matters most

questions.prompt: What does 'selective' mean in Mamba's selective state space model?
options: The model only attends to a random subset of tokens
Its B, C, and Δ parameters are functions of the current input, letting it decide per-token what to remember or forget
It selectively skips layers during inference to save compute
It selects which GPU to run on based on sequence length
questions.explanation: Earlier SSMs like S4 use fixed (linear time-invariant) parameters at every step. Mamba makes them input-dependent, giving content-aware, per-token filtering of what enters the state.

questions.prompt: What is Mamba's main complexity advantage over standard self-attention?
options: It needs no training data
Linear time and constant memory in sequence length, versus attention's quadratic time and growing KV cache
It eliminates the need for a GPU entirely
It has zero parameters
questions.explanation: Attention costs O(n²) time and its KV cache grows with sequence length; Mamba's fixed-size recurrent state gives O(n) time and constant memory regardless of context length.

questions.prompt: Where does plain attention still tend to beat plain Mamba?
options: Training throughput on short sequences
Exact recall of one specific fact buried deep in a very long context
Total parameter count
Energy efficiency at inference time
questions.explanation: Mamba compresses history into a fixed-size state, so a single old fact can get diluted or overwritten. Attention keeps uncompressed access to every past token, which is why hybrids like Jamba mix in some attention layers for precise recall.

references.title: Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces (arXiv:2312.00752)

posts.title: TimeLens2: One Model to Find Any Moment in Video
posts.subtitle: A single MLLM beats open-source rivals up to 397B parameters at knowing exactly when something happens on screen.
posts.summary: TimeLens2 is a family of multimodal LLMs (2B/4B/8B, built on Qwen3-VL) that localize exactly when an event happens in a video — short or long, single or repeated, third-person or egocentric. Trained with SFT on a re-verified dataset then GRPO with a new 'temporal Wasserstein reward,' the 8B model beats open-source models up to 397B parameters across seven benchmarks.

slides.title: Video Models Finally Learn to Say 'When'
slides.body: TimeLens2 is a family of MLLMs that pinpoint the exact time window an event happens in a video — not just whether it happened.

slides.title: Legacy Video Benchmarks Were Quietly Broken
bullets: Video temporal grounding (VTG) asks a model to return the start/end timestamps where a text query is visually true.
TimeLens2's authors rebuilt a benchmark from Charades-STA, ActivityNet Captions, and QVHighlights with verified annotations — and found models re-rank dramatically once label noise is removed.
That means prior 'state of the art' claims on the old benchmarks weren't reliably comparing the right thing.

slides.title: Two-Stage Recipe: Verified SFT, Then GRPO
bullets: Stage 1 (SFT): trains on a re-verified dataset that includes both single-interval AND multi-interval annotations (an event that happens more than once in a video).
Stage 2 (GRPO): reinforcement learning refines the model using a reward that combines temporal IoU with a new 'temporal Wasserstein reward.'

The Wasserstein reward computes the 1D Wasserstein (W1) distance between uniform distributions over the merged interval supports — giving dense feedback even when predicted and ground-truth interval counts don't match.

slides.title: Why a 'Matching-Free' Reward Matters
slides.body: Standard IoU rewards need predicted and true intervals matched one-to-one — awkward when an event repeats 3 times but the model predicts 2 or 4 windows. Treating both sets as distributions over time and measuring the distance between them sidesteps the matching problem entirely, while still rewarding partial credit.

slides.title: Bigger Isn't Always Better
bullets: Evaluated across seven temporal-grounding benchmarks.
Gains hold for both single-interval and repeated-event queries.

rows: State of the art in its size class
Beats open models up to 397B params

slides.title: Finding Every Time Someone Waves — Not Just One
slides.body: Ask 'when does the person wave at the camera' on a 10-minute egocentric video where it happens four times. A single-interval model returns one window and misses the rest; TimeLens2's multi-interval training lets it return all four, each with its own start/end timestamp.

slides.title: Where This Still Falls Short
bullets: Training depends on a curated, human-re-verified interval dataset — reproducing the gains on a new domain means redoing that verification work.
The re-ranking finding cuts both ways: a model that looked SOTA on the old Charades-STA/ActivityNet leaderboards may not actually be the best choice now.
It localizes time windows, not causal explanation — it tells you WHEN an event happens, not why.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: TimeLens2 (2B/4B/8B, built on Qwen3-VL) sets new state-of-the-art on video temporal grounding across seven benchmarks.
Its 8B model outperforms open-source models up to 397B parameters — proof that training signal quality can beat raw scale.
The core trick: a matching-free 'temporal Wasserstein reward' in a GRPO stage, after SFT on a re-verified, multi-interval dataset.
Code and models are open-sourced on GitHub (MCG-NJU/TimeLens2).

questions.prompt: What problem did the TimeLens2 team find with legacy VTG benchmarks like Charades-STA?
options: They were too small to train on
Label noise caused dramatic model re-ranking once cleaned up
They didn't include any video data
They only supported English queries
questions.explanation: The team rebuilt these benchmarks with verified annotations and found models ranked very differently once noisy labels were removed — meaning old leaderboards weren't a reliable signal of true performance.

questions.prompt: Why use a 'temporal Wasserstein reward' instead of plain IoU during RL training?
options: It's faster to compute than IoU
It works without needing to match predicted intervals to ground-truth ones one-to-one
It only works for single-interval events
It replaces the need for any labeled data
questions.explanation: IoU rewards typically require pairing each predicted interval with one ground-truth interval. The Wasserstein reward treats both sets as distributions over time and measures the distance between them directly, so it still gives useful signal when the counts don't match.

questions.prompt: What is the most notable performance result reported for TimeLens2-8B?
options: It runs entirely offline on a smartphone
It generates video instead of just analyzing it
It beats open-source models with up to 397B parameters despite being far smaller
It requires no training data at all
questions.explanation: TimeLens2-8B improves 18.1 mIoU points over its Qwen3-VL-8B backbone and surpasses open-source rivals roughly 50x its size — showing training recipe can matter more than parameter count.

references.title: TimeLens2: Generalist Video Temporal Grounding with Multimodal LLMs (arXiv 2607.17423)

posts.title: Claude Cowork Leaves the Desktop for Web and Mobile
posts.subtitle: Anthropic's own usage data says over 90% of Cowork sessions aren't code at all.
posts.summary: Anthropic expanded Claude Cowork from a desktop-only app to the web and mobile (iOS/Android), starting with Max subscribers. The bigger story is architectural: sessions now run remotely on Anthropic's servers, so scheduled and long-running tasks survive a device going offline — and usage data from 1.2 million sessions shows most people aren't using it to code.

slides.title: Cowork Escapes the Desktop
slides.body: Anthropic's agentic 'hand off the work' product now runs on web and mobile — and the usage data reveals who's actually using it.

slides.title: Cowork Was Desktop-Only — And Device-Bound
bullets: Claude Cowork launched as a desktop-app product where long-running tasks (email, calendar, file work) ran tied to that one machine.
If your laptop went to sleep or lost its connection, whatever Cowork was doing was at risk of stalling with it.
That's a real limit for tasks meant to run for hours — reconciling a spreadsheet, drafting a report, monitoring an inbox.

slides.title: Web, iOS, and Android — Running on Anthropic's Servers
bullets: Announced July 7, 2026: Cowork is rolling out to claude.ai on the web and to iOS/Android, in beta, starting with Max ($100/month) subscribers.
The new surfaces run as remote sessions hosted on Anthropic's infrastructure, not on your device — so a task (including a scheduled one) keeps running with no device online at all.
Chat and Cowork are unified into a single interface on web and desktop: one space, shared files, instead of two separate products.

slides.title: What People Actually Use It For
bullets: Based on Anthropic's analysis of 1.2 million Cowork sessions.
Business ops and content creation together account for roughly half of all usage.

rows: Business operations (reports, spend reconciliation, onboarding checklists)

slides.title: A Scheduled Task That Outlives Your Phone Screen
slides.body: Ask Cowork on mobile to pull this week's scattered Slack and email updates into a status report every Friday at 5pm. Because the session runs on Anthropic's servers rather than your phone, it fires on schedule and finishes even if your phone is off or the app is closed.

slides.title: What This Doesn't Change
bullets: Beta access starts with Max subscribers only — it isn't available to free or Pro users on launch day.
Remote execution means Cowork tasks touch your connected accounts and files on Anthropic's servers, not just locally — worth knowing before granting broad file or inbox access.
It's a rollout of reach (more surfaces), not a new model — the underlying agent capability is the same Cowork, now less tied to a single machine.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Cowork now runs on web (claude.ai) and mobile (iOS/Android), not just desktop — beta, Max subscribers first, rolling out over several weeks.
Anthropic's own data (1.2M sessions) shows over 90% of usage isn't coding: business operations (33.4%) and content/copywriting (16.4%) lead.
The real architecture shift: sessions run remotely on Anthropic's servers, so scheduled and long-running tasks survive your device going offline.
Chat and Cowork are merging into one interface — the 'agent vs. chatbot' distinction is fading.

questions.prompt: According to Anthropic's usage data, what share of Claude Cowork sessions are software development?
options: Over 90%
Roughly half
Under 10%
Exactly 33.4%
questions.explanation: Anthropic found the reverse of the common assumption: over 90% of the 1.2 million sessions analyzed were NOT software development — business operations (33.4%) and content creation (16.4%) led instead.

questions.prompt: What's the key architectural change enabling Cowork's web/mobile expansion?
options: Sessions now run in the browser sandbox for speed
Sessions run remotely on Anthropic's servers instead of the local device
The model was made smaller to fit on phones
Cowork now requires a constant device connection
questions.explanation: Moving execution to Anthropic's servers means a task — including a scheduled one — keeps running even with no device online at all, which is what makes a mobile or web-triggered long task actually reliable.

questions.prompt: Who gets access to the new web/mobile Cowork beta first?
options: All free-tier users
Enterprise customers only
Max plan ($100/month) subscribers
Only developers with API access
questions.explanation: The beta rolled out starting with Max subscribers, with broader plan access promised in the following weeks — a common staged-rollout pattern for compute-heavy agentic features.

posts.title: MCP Prompts: Reusable Workflows the User Chooses
posts.subtitle: The MCP primitive that turns a server's best workflow into a slash command, not a hidden tool call.
posts.summary: MCP has three server-side primitives: Tools, Resources, and Prompts. Prompts are named, parameterized templates that a user explicitly invokes (often as a slash command), letting a server package its best workflows deterministically instead of hoping the model reaches for the right tool at the right time.

slides.title: Not Every Good Workflow Should Be a Tool
slides.body: MCP has three server-side primitives — Tools, Resources, and Prompts. Prompts are the most overlooked, and the one built for humans to trigger on purpose.

slides.title: Tools Are Model-Controlled — Sometimes You Want User-Controlled
bullets: MCP Tools are invoked by the model's own judgment — the model decides when to call them based on the conversation.
But some workflows are too valuable, specific, or high-stakes to leave to the model's discretion every time — e.g. 'summarize this repo's open issues' or 'draft a release changelog.'
Without a dedicated primitive, servers either bloat their tool list with these, or bury them in documentation nobody reads.

slides.title: Prompts: Named, Parameterized Templates the Client Surfaces
bullets: A prompt is a named template with typed arguments, discovered via prompts/list and fetched via prompts/get.
Unlike a tool call, a prompt is meant to be user-controlled — MCP clients typically surface prompts as slash commands or a picker menu, not something the model reaches for silently.
prompts/get returns actual message content (potentially multiple messages, including embedded resources) that gets inserted straight into the conversation.

slides.title: Where Prompts Sit in the Protocol
bullets: Server declares a 'prompts' capability (with optional listChanged) during initialization.
Client calls prompts/list (paginated) to discover available prompts and their argument schemas.
Client calls prompts/get with the user-supplied arguments; the server returns the rendered message(s).
If a server declared listChanged, it sends notifications/prompts/list_changed whenever its available prompts change, so the client can refresh its menu.

slides.title: A 'Triage This PR' Slash Command
slides.body: A GitHub MCP server exposes a prompt named review-pr with one argument, pr_number. In a client like an IDE, a user types /review-pr 482 — the client calls prompts/get with {pr_number: 482}, and the server returns a fully-formed message bundling the diff, linked issues, and a review checklist, ready for the model to act on. The model never had to 'decide' to start a PR review; the user did, deterministically.

slides.title: Prompts vs. Tools vs. Resources

rows: Model calls a function mid-reasoning (e.g. run_query)
Static or dynamic data added to context (a file, a DB row)

slides.title: Where Prompts Fall Short
bullets: They're static templates with arguments — not a substitute for a tool when the model needs to decide dynamically whether and how to act.
Client support is inconsistent: many MCP clients render Tools and Resources well but don't yet have a polished UI for browsing and invoking Prompts.
Overusing Prompts for things that really need model judgment just relocates the same rigidity problem — you still need Tools for anything genuinely dynamic.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: MCP Prompts are named, parameterized templates fetched via prompts/list / prompts/get — designed for the USER to invoke, not the model.
They turn a server's best, most repeatable workflows into something like a slash command instead of hoping the model calls the right tool at the right time.
Use Prompts for curated, human-triggered workflows; keep Tools for anything the model must decide dynamically; use Resources for context that gets attached.
Client support varies — check whether your MCP client actually surfaces a server's prompts before building a workflow around them.

questions.prompt: What's the key difference between an MCP Tool and an MCP Prompt?
options: Tools are user-controlled, prompts are model-controlled
Tools are called by the model's own judgment; prompts are meant to be explicitly invoked by the user
Prompts can only return plain text, tools can return files
There is no functional difference, just naming
questions.explanation: Tools let the model decide when to call them during reasoning. Prompts are designed the other way around — a named, parameterized template the user (often via a slash command in the client UI) chooses to trigger on purpose.

questions.prompt: Which two MCP methods does a client use to work with prompts?
options: tools/call and tools/list
resources/read and resources/subscribe
prompts/list and prompts/get
sampling/createMessage and elicitation/create
questions.explanation: prompts/list discovers available prompts (with pagination), and prompts/get fetches the rendered message content for a specific prompt using the user-supplied arguments.

questions.prompt: A server's list of prompts changes at runtime (e.g. a plugin is installed). What should it do if it declared the listChanged capability?
options: Nothing — clients poll prompts/list on every turn
Send a notifications/prompts/list_changed notification so clients can refresh their menu
Restart the whole MCP connection
Silently drop the old prompts without notice
questions.explanation: Declaring listChanged is a promise: when the available prompts change, the server should proactively notify the client via notifications/prompts/list_changed, rather than making the client re-poll or discover the change by chance.

posts.title: SkillOpt: Turning an Agent's Skill File Into a Trained Parameter
posts.subtitle: Instead of fine-tuning weights, Microsoft Research trains the markdown skill document an agent reads — and wins on all 52 evaluated (model, benchmark, harness) cells.
posts.summary: SkillOpt (Microsoft Research) treats an agent's natural-language skill file as a trainable external parameter: a separate optimizer model turns scored rollouts into bounded add/delete/replace edits, accepting only edits that beat a held-out validation score. Across six benchmarks, seven target models, and three execution harnesses, it is best or tied-best on all 52 evaluated cells — without touching the target model's weights.

slides.title: The Skill File Is the Thing Being Trained, Not the Model
slides.body: SkillOpt reframes agent improvement: instead of asking 'how do we write a better prompt?', it asks 'how do we train the skill?'. The skill document — a plain markdown file the agent reads at run time — becomes the parameter that gets optimized, while the underlying LLM stays completely frozen.
bullets: Published by Microsoft Research, May 2026 (arXiv 2605.23904), with code and a best_skill.md artifact released on GitHub
Zero inference-time model calls at deployment — the optimization happens once, offline, before the skill file ships
Works across 7 target models, from frontier-scale GPT-5.5 down to small open-weight Qwen3.5-4B

slides.title: Manual Prompts and Fine-Tuning Both Fall Short
slides.body: Hand-written system prompts plateau because no human can enumerate every edge case an agent will hit. Fine-tuning the model fixes that but requires weight access, GPU budget, and redeployment — and it's overkill for behavior that's really just 'the agent needs better instructions.'
bullets: One-shot LLM-written skills and human-authored skills both stop improving after the first draft — there's no feedback loop from actual task outcomes
Fine-tuning ties the improvement to one specific model checkpoint; it doesn't transfer to a different backbone or a closed frontier API
Prior text-optimization baselines (TextGrad, GEPA, Trace2Skill, EvoSkill) edit prompts too, but SkillOpt's paper reports it beating all of them on every evaluated cell

slides.title: A Training Loop, Written in Text Instead of Gradients
slides.body: SkillOpt runs an explicit train/validate loop over the skill document: a separate optimizer model reads scored rollouts, proposes a bounded edit (add, delete, or replace a section), and the edit is kept only if it strictly improves a held-out validation score.
bullets: A 'textual learning-rate budget' caps how much the skill can change per round, preventing runaway edits from one noisy rollout
A rejected-edit buffer remembers edits that failed validation so the optimizer doesn't re-propose them
An epoch-wise slow/meta update consolidates many small accepted edits into a stable, readable skill file rather than a diff-riddled mess

slides.title: The Loop: Rollout, Score, Edit, Validate
slides.body: Each round: the frozen target model executes a task using the current skill file; the rollout is scored; the optimizer model proposes an edit to the skill based on where it fell short; the edited skill is checked against a held-out validation set before being accepted.
bullets: Target model (e.g. GPT-5.5) stays frozen the entire time — only the skill markdown changes
Validation gating means the skill file's performance is monotonic-ish across rounds: a bad edit simply doesn't ship
Tested across 3 execution harnesses — direct chat, Codex, and Claude Code — so the trained skill isn't tied to one agent runtime

slides.title: 52 Evaluated Cells, Best or Tied on All 52
slides.body: The paper counts every (target model, benchmark, harness) combination as one comparison cell, pitting SkillOpt against the strongest of no-skill, human-written skill, one-shot LLM skill, Trace2Skill, TextGrad, GEPA, and EvoSkill baselines.

slides.title: Worked Example: Spreadsheet Skill Goes From 41.8 to 80.7
slides.body: On SpreadsheetBench with GPT-5.5 in direct chat, the untrained skill scores 41.8. Across training rounds, the optimizer likely adds concrete formula-writing conventions and error-recovery steps the model kept missing, then validates each addition against held-out spreadsheet tasks.
bullets: A nearly 39-point jump on one benchmark is the largest single gain reported in the paper's six-benchmark suite
The average gain across all six benchmarks (direct chat, GPT-5.5) is +23.5 points — none of it from touching model weights
Because edits are validation-gated, the final best_skill.md reflects only changes that actually held up on unseen tasks, not just training-set overfitting

slides.title: Where This Approach Runs Into Limits
slides.body: Text-space optimization trades one set of constraints for another — it's cheap to deploy but not free to produce, and it inherits the ceiling of what a skill document can express.
bullets: Training still costs optimizer-model calls and scored rollouts per round — 'zero inference-time calls' refers to deployment, not the optimization process itself
A single skill document is a bottleneck: behaviors that need actual new capability (not better instructions) still require fine-tuning or a different model
Don't reach for this when you lack a scorable task signal — the validation-gating that makes SkillOpt reliable depends on being able to grade a rollout automatically
Skip it for one-off prompts; the training loop pays off when the same agent skill will run thousands of times across varied inputs

slides.title: The Reusable Idea: Some Behavior Belongs in Text, Not Weights
bullets: SkillOpt shows a markdown skill file can be optimized with the same train/validate discipline as model weights — propose an edit, check it against held-out data, keep it only if it wins
The output is a portable artifact (best_skill.md) that transfers across models and agent harnesses, unlike a fine-tuned checkpoint
It's a strong option when you have a frozen or closed model, a scorable task, and behavior that's really an instruction-following gap rather than a missing capability

questions.prompt: What is actually being optimized in SkillOpt — and what stays frozen?
options: The target model's weights are fine-tuned while the skill file stays fixed
The skill document (a plain markdown file) is edited by a separate optimizer model, while the target model's weights never change
Both the target model and the skill file are updated simultaneously via joint gradient descent
A new model is distilled from the target model's rollouts
questions.explanation: SkillOpt's core move is treating the skill file as the trainable parameter living outside a frozen target model — the optimizer model proposes edits to that document, never to the target model's weights.

questions.prompt: What stops SkillOpt from accepting a bad edit to the skill file?
options: Edits are only accepted if a human reviewer approves them
An edit is kept only if it strictly improves a held-out validation score; a textual learning-rate budget also caps how much can change per round
The optimizer model can only ever add text, never delete or replace it
Edits are accepted randomly and averaged over many rounds
questions.explanation: Validation gating is the safeguard: a proposed add/delete/replace edit only ships if it beats the current skill on a held-out set, and a bounded 'textual learning-rate budget' prevents any single round from over-editing based on noisy feedback.

questions.prompt: Why would SkillOpt be a poor fit for optimizing a one-off, never-repeated prompt?
options: It only works on coding tasks
It requires GPU fine-tuning infrastructure
The train/validate loop needs scored rollouts across many task instances to gate edits reliably — a one-off prompt gives no repeated signal to train against
It can only edit prompts written in English
questions.explanation: SkillOpt's benefit comes from running many rollouts against a benchmark or task distribution and validating edits against held-out cases. A skill that will only ever be used once has no repeated signal to learn from, so the training loop has nothing to optimize against.

references.title: SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills (arXiv 2605.23904)

posts.title: Gemini 3.6 Flash: Google Cuts Tokens Without Cutting Capability
posts.subtitle: A workhorse-tier refresh that spends 17% fewer output tokens per task and prices output 17% cheaper — while two companion models chase throughput and security.
posts.summary: On July 21, 2026, Google shipped Gemini 3.6 Flash alongside two companions — 3.5 Flash-Lite and 3.5 Flash Cyber. The headline isn't a benchmark record; it's efficiency: 17% fewer output tokens than 3.5 Flash on the Artificial Analysis Index, lower per-token pricing, and measurably fewer wasted tool calls in agentic coding.

slides.title: Three Models, One Theme: Do More Per Token
slides.body: Google announced Gemini 3.6 Flash, Gemini 3.5 Flash-Lite, and Gemini 3.5 Flash Cyber together on July 21, 2026, available immediately via the Gemini API and Google AI Studio.
bullets: 3.6 Flash: the general workhorse — better coding, knowledge work, and multimodal performance than 3.5 Flash
3.5 Flash-Lite: the fastest, most cost-effective 3.5-class model, built for high-throughput tasks like agentic search
3.5 Flash Cyber: 3.5 Flash fine-tuned specifically to find and fix security vulnerabilities in code

slides.title: Agentic Workloads Are Bottlenecked by Token Spend, Not Just IQ
slides.body: As more products run agent loops (plan, call a tool, observe, repeat), the cost that matters most in production often isn't a benchmark score — it's how many tokens and tool calls it takes to finish a task, since every wasted loop is billed.
bullets: A model that's marginally smarter but takes 3x the tool calls to finish a task can cost more in production than a slightly weaker, more efficient one
3.5 Flash's $9/1M output token price made heavy agentic usage add up quickly at scale
Google needed a workhorse tier that reduces reasoning steps and tool calls per task, not just raw accuracy

slides.title: What Actually Changed in 3.6 Flash
slides.body: Per Google's announcement, 3.6 Flash uses measurably fewer output tokens and fewer reasoning/tool-call steps to accomplish the same multi-step workflows as 3.5 Flash.
bullets: 17% fewer output tokens than 3.5 Flash on the Artificial Analysis Index — a cross-provider efficiency benchmark
On DeepSWE (Datacurve's coding benchmark), output token usage drops by up to 65%, with higher precision and fewer unwanted code edits
Supports text, images, audio, and video input, with a context window up to 1 million tokens

slides.title: Pricing: Flash Gets Cheaper, Flash-Lite Gets a Refresh
Worked Example: An Agentic Coding Task
slides.body: Take a multi-step coding task — read a bug report, locate the file, edit it, run tests, and fix a follow-up failure. On DeepSWE, 3.6 Flash reaches 49% precision (fewer unwanted edits and execution loops) versus 37% for 3.5 Flash.
bullets: Fewer 'unwanted code edits' means fewer wasted attempts that still get billed as output tokens
Reduced execution loops directly cuts the number of tool calls (test runs, file reads) a coding agent needs per task
Combined with the 17%+ token reduction and lower output pricing, a comparable coding-agent workload gets cheaper from two directions at once: fewer tokens, and a lower price per token

slides.title: Where the Flash Tier Still Isn't the Right Choice
slides.body: None of these three models replace a frontier reasoning tier — Google explicitly frames 3.6 Flash as the workhorse, not the flagship.
bullets: Gemini 3.5 Pro — the higher-reasoning tier — is still not broadly available; Google says it's coming soon, so tasks needing top-tier reasoning quality aren't 3.6 Flash's job
Gemini 4 is only in pre-training, per Google's own announcement — there's no near-term frontier jump to expect from this release cycle
Flash Cyber is scoped to code security scanning/patching — it isn't a general-purpose security or pentesting tool

slides.title: The Real Story Is Cost-Per-Completed-Task, Not Cost-Per-Token
bullets: 3.6 Flash's gain isn't a new capability — it's doing the same job in fewer tokens and fewer tool-call loops, which compounds with a lower per-token price
The three-model split (Flash, Flash-Lite, Flash Cyber) signals Google optimizing for workload fit rather than one model to serve everything
For agentic/coding workloads specifically, precision-per-loop (fewer unwanted edits) matters as much as raw benchmark accuracy, since it directly drives billed token volume

questions.prompt: What is the headline efficiency claim Google makes for Gemini 3.6 Flash versus 3.5 Flash?
options: It has a 10x larger context window
It uses 17% fewer output tokens on the Artificial Analysis Index, with up to 65% fewer output tokens on the DeepSWE coding benchmark
It is the first Gemini model to support video input
It replaces Gemini 3.5 Pro as Google's flagship reasoning model
questions.explanation: The release is framed around efficiency, not a new capability tier: 17% fewer output tokens on a general cross-provider index, and up to 65% fewer on the DeepSWE coding benchmark specifically — plus a lower output price per token.

questions.prompt: In an agentic coding workload, why does 'fewer unwanted code edits' matter for cost, not just quality?
options: Unwanted edits are free but slow
Each unwanted edit and extra execution loop consumes and bills additional output tokens and tool calls, so higher precision-per-loop directly lowers total spend
It only affects the model's training cost, not inference cost
It has no effect on billed usage
questions.explanation: In an agent loop, every wasted edit or extra test run is additional billed output and another tool call. 3.6 Flash's reported 49% vs 37% precision on DeepSWE means fewer of those wasted loops, which compounds with the lower per-token price.

questions.prompt: Which task would 3.6 Flash NOT be the right tool for, per Google's own framing?
options: A high-throughput agentic search pipeline
A multi-step coding agent fixing bugs and running tests
A task requiring Google's top-tier reasoning quality, since Gemini 3.5 Pro is still not broadly available and 3.6 Flash is positioned as the workhorse tier
A document understanding task with images and long context
questions.explanation: Google explicitly frames 3.6 Flash as the efficient workhorse tier, not the top-end reasoning tier — that's Gemini 3.5 Pro, which the announcement says is still not broadly available.

references.title: Introducing Gemini 3.6 Flash, 3.5 Flash-Lite, and 3.5 Flash Cyber — Google Blog

posts.title: MCP Resources: Giving Models Context Without a Tool Call
posts.subtitle: Tools let an agent take actions. Resources let a server hand over data the model can read — addressed by URI, opt-in from the client side, and updatable via subscriptions.
posts.summary: MCP's Resources primitive lets a server expose URI-addressed data — files, schemas, records — that a client can read and attach to a conversation, separate from Tools (callable actions) and Prompts (templates). Resource templates parameterize dynamic data, and optional subscriptions let a client stay in sync when a resource changes.

slides.title: Not Every Piece of Context Should Be a Tool Call
slides.body: MCP defines three primitives: Tools (functions the model can invoke), Prompts (server-managed templates), and Resources — URI-addressed data a client can read and decide whether to attach.
bullets: A resource is identified by a URI, e.g. file:///project/README.md or postgres://db/schema/users
Reading a resource is a client-driven decision — the model doesn't call it like a tool; the host application chooses what to attach
Resources can be static (a fixed file) or dynamic (a live query result), and can carry a MIME type

slides.title: Without Resources, Context Gets Stuffed Into Tool Results or Prompts
slides.body: Before a dedicated data primitive, the only ways to get information in front of a model were baking it into the system prompt or wrapping every read as a fake 'tool call,' both of which blur the line between taking an action and just looking something up.
bullets: Treating a read-only lookup as a tool call adds an unnecessary round trip and forces the model to decide when to fetch data it might just need by default
Static system-prompt stuffing doesn't scale — you can't cram an entire codebase or database schema into every request
Users and host applications had no standard way to browse and selectively attach 'what's available' before a request was made

slides.title: Resources: List, Read, and Optionally Template
slides.body: A server declares the resources capability and implements resources/list (what's available) and resources/read (fetch one by URI). Resource templates let a server expose parameterized families of resources using URI templates instead of enumerating every instance.
bullets: resources/list returns available resources (and/or resource templates) with their URI, name, and MIME type
A template like file:///{path} or repo://{owner}/{repo}/{path} lets a client construct a valid URI on demand rather than requiring the server to list every file up front
resources/read takes a concrete URI and returns the resource's contents (text or binary, base64-encoded)

slides.title: Subscriptions Keep the Client in Sync
slides.body: Resources support two independent optional capabilities: listChanged (the set of available resources can change, and the server notifies clients) and subscribe (a client asks to be notified when one specific resource's contents change).
bullets: A client calls resources/subscribe with a URI; when that resource's content changes, the server sends a notifications/resources/updated notification
The client then re-reads the resource rather than the server pushing the new content directly — subscriptions notify, they don't stream
Servers can support neither, either, or both of listChanged/subscribe — a static file server may need neither

slides.title: Worked Example: A Repo-Browsing MCP Server
slides.body: A Git MCP server exposes a resource template repo://{path} instead of hard-coding every file. A code-review host application lists resources, sees the template, and lets a user pick specific files to attach to the conversation as context — no tool call involved.
bullets: User expands a file tree in the host UI, which resolves repo://src/auth/login.py to a concrete URI and calls resources/read
If the user then edits that file locally and the server watches the filesystem, it can fire notifications/resources/updated for that URI if the client subscribed
The model never 'decided' to read the file — the human, via the host application, controlled exactly what got attached

slides.title: Tools vs Resources vs Prompts

rows: The model, via a tool call
The client/host, on the user's behalf
Often yes (can act on the world)
Send an email, run a query, call an API
Attach a file, schema, or record as context

slides.title: When Resources Are the Wrong Primitive
slides.body: Resources are deliberately inert — no side effects, no model-triggered invocation — which is exactly where they stop being useful.
bullets: If the model needs to decide at run time whether to fetch something based on reasoning mid-task, that's a Tool, not a Resource — resources are attached ahead of or alongside a request, not called by the model on demand
If the action changes state (writes a file, sends a request), it must be a Tool — Resources have no write path
For very large or frequently changing datasets, exposing a search/query Tool is often more efficient than listing thousands of individual resource URIs

slides.title: Resources Separate 'What the Model Can See' From 'What It Can Do'
bullets: Tools are actions the model chooses to invoke; Resources are data a host application chooses to attach — keeping that boundary explicit avoids conflating lookups with side effects
URI templates let a server expose a whole class of data (files, records) without enumerating every instance up front
subscribe/listChanged keep long-lived context fresh without forcing the client to poll resources/read on a timer

questions.prompt: What is the key difference in how Tools versus Resources get invoked in MCP?
options: Tools and Resources are invoked identically — there's no functional difference
The model calls Tools directly during reasoning; Resources are read and attached by the client/host application, not invoked by the model as an action
Resources can only be used by administrators, while Tools are available to any user
Tools are read-only while Resources can modify state
questions.explanation: Tools are actions the model decides to call mid-task. Resources are data the host application (often on the user's behalf) chooses to read and attach as context — the model doesn't invoke a resource read the way it invokes a tool call.

questions.prompt: What problem do resource templates (e.g. file:///{path}) solve?
options: They encrypt resource contents in transit
They let a server expose a parameterized family of resources without having to enumerate every individual URI in resources/list
They allow resources to have side effects like Tools
They replace the need for a MIME type on each resource
questions.explanation: Without templates, a server would need to list every possible file or record as a separate resource. A URI template lets the client construct a valid, specific URI on demand — e.g. resolving a chosen file path — rather than the server pre-listing everything.

questions.prompt: A client calls resources/subscribe on a URI. What happens when that resource's content changes?
options: The server immediately streams the new content to the client over the same connection
Nothing — subscriptions only affect resources/list, not resources/read
The server sends a notifications/resources/updated notification, and the client is expected to re-read the resource to get the new content
The resource's URI changes to reflect the new version
questions.explanation: MCP subscriptions are notify-then-pull: the server notifies the client that a subscribed resource changed, but the client must call resources/read again to fetch the updated contents — the server doesn't push the content itself.

posts.title: Direct Preference Optimization: Skip the Reward Model Entirely
posts.subtitle: RLHF needs a reward model and an RL loop to align a language model. DPO proves you can get the same result with one closed-form loss and no RL at all.
posts.summary: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) reframes RLHF's reward-then-RL pipeline as a single classification-style loss computed directly on preference pairs. By exploiting a closed-form relationship between optimal policies and rewards under a KL constraint, it trains a language model to prefer better answers without ever training a separate reward model or running PPO rollouts.

slides.title: The Same RLHF Result, Without Reinforcement Learning
slides.body: RLHF aligns a language model to human preferences in two stages: train a reward model on comparison data, then run PPO to maximize that reward while staying close to the original model. DPO (Rafailov et al., Stanford, 2023) collapses both stages into one supervised-style loss trained directly on the preference pairs.
bullets: No reward model to train, no PPO rollouts to sample, no critic/value network — just gradient descent on (prompt, chosen, rejected) triples
Mathematically equivalent objective to RLHF's reward-maximization-under-KL-constraint problem — DPO doesn't approximate RLHF, it reparameterizes it
Became a default alignment recipe for many open-weight model releases within months of publication

slides.title: RLHF's Reward-Then-RL Pipeline Is Expensive and Fragile
slides.body: Classic RLHF needs several models in memory during training — the policy, a frozen reference copy for the KL penalty, a reward model, and PPO's critic. Each stage introduces its own instability.
bullets: Stage 1: train a reward model on human preference pairs — it can be exploited ('reward hacking') if it doesn't generalize past its training distribution
Stage 2: run PPO, sampling new completions from the current policy every step, scoring them with the reward model, and updating with a clipped policy-gradient objective
PPO's own hyperparameters (clip range, KL coefficient, batch size, PPO epochs per rollout) are notoriously fragile to tune at LLM scale

slides.title: The Reward Model Is Already Implicit in the Policy
slides.body: DPO's key trick: under a KL-constrained reward-maximization objective, the optimal policy has a closed form — pi*(y|x) is proportional to pi_ref(y|x) times exp(r(x,y)/beta). That lets you solve for the reward in terms of the policy itself, and substitute it directly into the Bradley-Terry preference likelihood.
bullets: The result is one loss: L = -log(sigmoid(beta * [(log pi(yw|x) - log pi_ref(yw|x)) - (log pi(yl|x) - log pi_ref(yl|x))])), computed on preferred (yw) vs rejected (yl) completions
beta plays the same role as the KL penalty coefficient in PPO — it controls how far the tuned policy is allowed to drift from the reference
Because it's just a per-example loss (no rollouts), training looks like ordinary supervised fine-tuning on a fixed dataset of triples

slides.title: What the Loss Actually Rewards and Punishes
slides.body: The gradient of the DPO loss increases the log-probability of the chosen response and decreases the log-probability of the rejected one, weighted by how wrong the model's implicit reward currently is.
bullets: Implicit reward for a response: beta * log(pi(y|x) / pi_ref(y|x)) — how much more likely the current policy makes y versus the frozen reference
If the model already strongly prefers yw over yl, the sigmoid saturates and the gradient weight shrinks — the loss stops pushing once the ranking is confidently correct
Training data is entirely offline: static (prompt, chosen, rejected) triples collected once, with no need to sample from the live policy during training

slides.title: PPO vs GRPO vs DPO: What Each One Needs at Training Time

rows: No — reward is implicit in the policy
Any reward signal, incl. RL environments

slides.title: Worked Example: One Preference Pair
slides.body: Prompt: 'Explain recursion to a beginner.' Chosen (yw): a short answer with a concrete base-case example. Rejected (yl): a dense, jargon-heavy paragraph with no example.
bullets: Compute log pi(yw|x) and log pi(yl|x) under the current policy, and the same two values under the frozen reference model — four log-probabilities total, no new generation required since yw/yl are fixed text
margin = beta * [(log pi(yw) - log pi_ref(yw)) - (log pi(yl) - log pi_ref(yl))]
If that margin is already large and positive, sigmoid saturates near 1, loss is near 0, and this pair contributes almost no gradient — the model has already 'learned' this comparison
With beta = 0.1 (a common default), the policy is kept close to the reference; raising beta trades alignment strength for more drift from the reference's original fluency

slides.title: Where DPO Breaks Down
slides.body: DPO trades RL's complexity for a new set of failure modes rooted in its offline, closed-form nature.
bullets: Fully offline: it can only rank completions that already exist in the dataset — it never explores new completions the way PPO's online sampling does, so it can't discover better responses outside the preference data's distribution
Likelihood displacement: pushing down a rejected response's probability can inadvertently push down probability mass on similar, even correct, completions if the training pairs aren't diverse
Sensitive to reference-policy quality: since the implicit reward is defined relative to pi_ref, starting from a poorly fine-tuned reference model biases every subsequent preference judgment

Not a fix for reward hacking on its own: an under-specified or biased preference dataset teaches the model to exploit whatever pattern separates chosen from rejected in that data, the same risk a bad reward model carries

slides.title: Same Objective, Different Optimization Path
bullets: DPO is not a heuristic approximation of RLHF — it's a reparameterization that reaches the same KL-constrained optimum via supervised-style gradient descent

Removing the reward model and RL loop removes an enormous amount of training infrastructure — but also removes online exploration, which matters when preference data doesn't cover the response space you care about

Use DPO when you have a solid preference dataset and want a stable, RL-free path to alignment; prefer online RL (PPO/GRPO) when rewards are verifiable and the policy needs to explore beyond a fixed dataset

questions.prompt: What closed-form relationship does DPO exploit to eliminate the separate reward model?
options: It assumes all rewards are zero and trains only on log-likelihood

Under a KL-constrained reward-maximization objective, the optimal policy is proportional to pi_ref(y|x) * exp(r(x,y)/beta) — so the reward can be solved for in terms of the policy and substituted into the preference likelihood
It distills the reward model directly into the policy's weights via knowledge distillation
It replaces the reward model with a majority vote across sampled completions
questions.explanation: DPO's derivation starts from the same KL-constrained objective RLHF optimizes, shows the optimal policy has this closed form, algebraically isolates the reward, and plugs it into the Bradley-Terry preference model — producing a loss defined purely in terms of policy log-probabilities.

questions.prompt: Why is DPO training described as 'offline'?
options: It only works without an internet connection
It trains on a fixed dataset of (prompt, chosen, rejected) triples and never needs to sample new completions from the live policy during training, unlike PPO's online rollouts
It can only be run overnight due to compute cost
It doesn't require GPUs
questions.explanation: PPO needs to generate fresh completions from the current policy at every training step to score with the reward model. DPO's loss only needs log-probabilities of already-fixed chosen/rejected text, so the whole training set can be prepared once, upfront.

questions.prompt: What is a known failure mode of DPO that online RL methods are less prone to?
options: It requires more GPU memory than PPO
It cannot be combined with a KL penalty
Likelihood displacement — suppressing a rejected response's probability can also suppress similar, even correct, completions, since DPO never explores new responses beyond the fixed dataset
It only works on summarization tasks
questions.explanation: Because DPO only ever compares completions that already exist in the training data, its gradient updates can generalize in unintended ways to nearby completions never seen during training — a risk online methods mitigate by continually sampling fresh completions from the current policy.
