posts.title: Tree of Thoughts: Search Instead of a Single Chain
posts.subtitle: Chain-of-thought commits to one reasoning path and never looks back. Tree of Thoughts turns generation into search — with backtracking.
posts.summary: Tree of Thoughts (Yao et al., NeurIPS 2023) reframes LLM reasoning as search over a tree of intermediate 'thoughts': generate several candidate next steps, have the model score each one, and use BFS/DFS to keep the promising branches and backtrack from dead ends. On Game of 24, this lifted GPT-4's success rate from 4% (chain-of-thought) to 74% — at the cost of dozens to hundreds of extra model calls, so it pays off only on tasks with real lookahead or backtracking needs.

slides.body: Standard prompting generates one linear chain of tokens, left to right, with no way to reconsider an early misstep. Tree of Thoughts lets the model explore multiple paths, evaluate them, and backtrack — like a search algorithm instead of a single guess.
slides.title: What If the Model Could Reconsider?

bullets: CoT commits to one reasoning path token-by-token, left to right, with no mechanism to reconsider an early misstep
Tasks needing lookahead or backtracking — planning, constrained puzzles, multi-step math — expose this: one bad early choice dooms the whole chain
Self-consistency (sample N full chains, majority-vote the final answer) helps, but it only evaluates complete answers, never partial progress along the way
slides.title: Chain-of-Thought's Blind Spot

bullets: Thought decomposition: break the task into intermediate steps ('thoughts') small enough to evaluate, large enough to be meaningful
Thought generator: propose several distinct next thoughts from the current state, either by independent sampling or by asking for k continuations
State evaluator: the LLM itself scores or votes on each new state's promise (e.g. classify 'sure / maybe / impossible') — no separate reward model needed
Search algorithm: BFS keeps the top-b promising states per level; DFS explores one path deeply and backtracks on failure
slides.title: Four Pieces That Turn Generation Into Search

bullets: State = the shorter list of numbers remaining after one operation
The thought generator proposes several valid next operations per state — e.g. from [4, 9, 10, 13]: '13-9=4 -> [4,4,10]' or '10-4=6 -> [6,9,13]'
The state evaluator prompts the model to judge each resulting set as 'sure / maybe / impossible' of reaching 24
BFS with breadth b=5 keeps only the 5 most promising states per step, discards dead ends, and backtracks when a branch is judged impossible
slides.body: Given 4 numbers, combine them with +, -, x, / to reach exactly 24.
slides.title: Worked Example: Game of 24

slides.body: GPT-4 success rate on the Game of 24 benchmark, from the original paper.
slides.title: The Headline Result

bullets: Cost multiplies: solving one Game of 24 puzzle with ToT can take dozens of LLM calls (proposals + evaluations) versus a handful for plain CoT — real dollar and latency cost
Self-evaluation isn't calibrated: the same model that generates thoughts also grades them, so evaluator bias or hallucination can prune good branches or keep bad ones
Diminishing returns on easy tasks: if a single CoT chain already gets the right answer reliably (simple arithmetic, factual QA), ToT's extra search adds cost without lifting accuracy
Needs a decomposable task: ToT shines when a problem has clear, partially-evaluable intermediate states (puzzles, planning) — it doesn't help open-ended generation with no clear 'good state' signal
slides.title: Tradeoffs: When Search Isn't Worth It

bullets: Tree of Thoughts reframes LLM reasoning as search: generate multiple next steps, evaluate them, keep the promising ones, backtrack on dead ends
It turns single-shot generation into deliberate, backtrackable problem solving — at the cost of many more LLM calls per answer
Reach for it on tasks with clear intermediate states and real payoff for lookahead: planning, constrained puzzles, multi-step math — not tasks a single well-prompted chain already nails
slides.title: Key Takeaways

questions.prompt: What is the core difference between standard Chain-of-Thought prompting and Tree of Thoughts?
options: CoT uses a larger model; ToT uses a smaller one
CoT commits to a single linear reasoning path; ToT searches and evaluates multiple candidate paths, backtracking when needed
ToT requires fine-tuning the model; CoT does not
They are the same technique with different names
questions.explanation: CoT generates one uninterrupted chain of reasoning tokens with no way to reconsider a step. ToT decomposes the problem into evaluable 'thoughts,' generates several candidates per step, scores them, and uses BFS/DFS to backtrack away from dead ends.

questions.prompt: In the original Tree of Thoughts paper's Game of 24 benchmark, roughly what success rate did GPT-4 achieve with standard Chain-of-Thought prompting versus ToT with breadth b=5?
options: CoT ~4% vs ToT ~74%
CoT ~50% vs ToT ~55%
CoT ~90% vs ToT ~95%
CoT ~4% vs ToT ~10%
questions.explanation: The gap is dramatic because Game of 24 needs lookahead and backtracking — exactly what a single linear CoT chain can't do but tree search can, by exploring and pruning multiple partial solutions.

questions.prompt: What is the biggest practical tradeoff of using Tree of Thoughts instead of plain prompting?
options: It only works with open-source models
It requires a separately trained reward model to score states
It multiplies LLM calls and cost/latency, since every candidate thought and every state evaluation is its own model call
It cannot be combined with few-shot examples
questions.explanation: Every thought proposal and every state evaluation ('sure/maybe/impossible' or a vote) is a separate LLM call, so ToT can require dozens of calls per problem — worth it only when the accuracy gain justifies the extra cost.

references.title: Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models (Yao et al., NeurIPS 2023)

posts.title: Test-Time Compute: Making Models Think Longer, Not Just Bigger
posts.subtitle: Why the newest reasoning models spend seconds (or minutes) computing before they answer — and how that trades latency for accuracy.
posts.summary: Test-time compute lets a model trade extra inference-time work — longer reasoning, multiple sampled attempts, or search over candidate solutions — for higher accuracy, instead of only relying on a bigger pretrained model. It's powerful on hard, verifiable problems but wasteful on easy ones, so production systems route by difficulty.

slides.title: Two Ways to Make a Model Smarter
slides.body: For years, 'smarter' meant more parameters and more training data. Reasoning models added a second lever: spend more compute per answer, at inference time.

slides.title: Train-Time Scaling Hits Diminishing Returns
slides.body: Scaling laws show accuracy improves with model size and training compute, but each doubling costs more and yields less. A model with more parameters also answers every query at the same fixed cost — a one-line question and a multi-step proof get identical compute.
bullets: Fixed forward pass = fixed thinking budget, no matter how hard the question
Bigger pretraining runs cost more to train AND more to serve, forever
Some problems (multi-step math, debugging, planning) need iteration, not just recall

slides.title: Spend Compute Where It's Needed: At Answer Time
slides.body: Test-time compute keeps the model fixed and instead lets it do more work per query: generate a longer chain of thought, produce multiple candidate answers, or search over reasoning paths before committing to a final response.
bullets: Sequential scaling: let the model reason step-by-step for longer before answering
Parallel scaling: sample N independent attempts, then pick the best one
Search-guided scaling: use a verifier or reward model to steer which reasoning branches to keep exploring

slides.title: Three Concrete Techniques
slides.body: These aren't mutually exclusive — production reasoning systems often combine them.
bullets: Extended chain-of-thought: the model writes out intermediate reasoning tokens before the final answer, effectively 'thinking out loud'
Self-consistency / best-of-N: sample the model many times at nonzero temperature, then take a majority vote (or rerank with a verifier) over the final answers
Tree/beam search over reasoning: branch at each reasoning step, score partial paths with a verifier, and prune weak branches — closer to how a person double-checks work

slides.title: Train-Time vs. Test-Time Scaling
slides.body: Neither replaces the other — they're different knobs with different cost profiles.

rows: Only the queries you spend it on
Wasteful on easy queries; adds latency

slides.title: Worked Example: A Tricky Coding Bug
slides.body: Ask a model to fix a race condition in concurrent code.
bullets: Single-pass answer: model pattern-matches to a common fix, misses the actual race window, ships a plausible-looking but wrong patch
With test-time compute: model drafts a fix, reasons through a step-by-step trace of thread interleavings, notices the draft still allows a bad interleaving, revises, and only then outputs the patch
With self-consistency: the model is sampled 5 times independently; 4 of 5 attempts converge on the same root cause, which is a strong signal the diagnosis is correct even without running the code

slides.title: Tradeoffs and When Not to Use It
slides.body: More thinking isn't free, and it isn't always helpful.
bullets: Latency cost: a query that took 1 second can take 10-60+ seconds with heavy reasoning or multi-sample voting
Compute cost multiplies directly with samples/search width — best-of-32 is roughly 32x the inference cost of one pass
Diminishing returns: on problems the model already answers reliably in one pass, extra thinking adds cost with near-zero accuracy gain
Needs a way to judge quality: self-consistency and search work best when answers are checkable (math, code, structured tasks) — much weaker signal on open-ended writing or subjective questions

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Test-time compute trades inference cost for accuracy by letting a model reason longer, sample more, or search over candidate answers
It is a second scaling axis alongside model/data size — not a replacement for it
It shines on hard, verifiable problems (math, code, planning) and struggles to pay off on easy or unverifiable ones
Real systems route by difficulty: cheap fast pass for easy queries, heavier reasoning budget only when needed

questions.prompt: What is 'test-time compute' scaling, in contrast to traditional model scaling?
options: Training on a larger dataset before deployment
Spending more inference-time computation per query (longer reasoning, more samples, or search) instead of only growing the model
Running the model on faster GPUs at inference
Compressing the model with quantization to reduce serving cost
questions.explanation: Test-time (inference-time) compute keeps the trained model fixed and instead lets it do more work per query — reasoning longer, sampling multiple attempts, or searching over paths — trading latency/cost for accuracy on that specific query.

questions.prompt: Why does self-consistency (best-of-N with majority voting) work particularly well on math and code problems?
options: Because math and code answers are shorter than other text
Because those domains have a checkable notion of correctness, so independent correct attempts tend to converge on the same answer while wrong attempts scatter
Because it only works when temperature is set to zero
Because math and code require less reasoning than other tasks
questions.explanation: When there is a verifiable right answer, sampling multiple independent attempts and taking the majority vote works because correct reasoning paths tend to agree, while errors are more randomly distributed — a much weaker signal on subjective or open-ended tasks.

questions.prompt: Why might a production system skip test-time compute for a simple factual query like "what is the capital of France"?
options: Because reasoning models can't answer factual questions
Because the extra latency and multiplied inference cost buy almost no accuracy gain on a query the model already answers reliably in one pass
Because test-time compute only works on multiple-choice questions
Because factual queries require training-time scaling instead
questions.explanation: Test-time compute has diminishing returns on problems the model already solves reliably — spending 10-60x more latency and compute for a negligible accuracy bump is a bad tradeoff, so systems typically route easy queries to a fast single-pass answer.

posts.title: From Pixels to States: Rethinking World Models as Game Engines
posts.subtitle: The #1 trending paper on Hugging Face this week argues video world models should simulate state, then render — not just predict pixels.
posts.summary: Most interactive world models generate the next frame from pixels and an action, with no explicit game state carried forward — so long rollouts silently drift (health bars 'heal', positions slip). This paper reframes the problem around how real game engines work: simulate authoritative state first, render pixels second. It's backed by a purpose-built dataset of 90+ hours of Black Myth: Wukong footage with frame-aligned actions and ground-truth state, and was the most-upvoted paper on Hugging Face Daily Papers for July 19, 2026.

slides.body: Most 'playable' video-generation world models predict pixels frame by frame, with no notion of health, position, or which attack just landed. This paper argues that's backwards: treat the model like a game engine, not a camera.
slides.title: A Video Generator That Forgot It Was Playing a Game

bullets: Diffusion/video world models generate the next frame conditioned on pixels plus an action, with no explicit game state carried forward between frames.
Without a state channel, small per-frame errors compound: a character's HP, position, or which enemies are alive can silently drift from what actually happened in the rollout.
Long rollouts (minutes, not seconds) break this hardest — the model has no ground truth to re-anchor to, unlike a real game engine that stores authoritative state and only renders it.
Most training datasets for these models pair frames only with pixels and actions, not the actual internal game state, so there's nothing to supervise state-tracking against even if you wanted to.
slides.title: Pixels-Only World Models Drift and Forget

bullets: Four axes the model is built and evaluated around: player action control, game-state dynamics, state–observation persistence, and real-time generation.

State–observation persistence is the key addition — the model is pushed to keep an explicit, checkable game-state representation across the rollout, not just whatever the pixels happen to imply, and render observations from that state.

This mirrors a real game engine: a simulation step updates authoritative state (HP, position, cooldowns); rendering is a separate, later step that reads that state — so visual drift can't silently corrupt the game logic.
slides.body: The paper organizes an interactive world model along a real game engine's own separation of concerns: simulate state, then render.
slides.title: Treat State as First-Class, Pixels as the Last Step

bullets: 90+ hours of gameplay footage captured with frame-aligned actions, ground-truth game state, and visual observations, all with structured annotations.
Frame-aligned actions means every input (attack, dodge, cast) is timestamped to the exact frame it affected — supervision a pixels-only model never had access to.

The same research group previously released WildWorld, a related open dataset (100M+ annotated frames, hundreds of hours, 450+ semantic actions) for action-conditioned world modeling — this paper extends that state-centric approach to a full game-engine framing.
slides.body: To train and evaluate this, the authors built a data pipeline around the action game Black Myth: Wukong.
slides.title: A Purpose-Built Data Engine: Black Myth Wukong

bullets: A pixels-only model asked to generate a long boss fight will often let the boss's health visually 'heal' after a hit lands, because nothing but pixel continuity is tracking damage.

A state-aware, game-engine-style model instead decrements an explicit HP value on a landed hit, and pixels are generated to stay consistent with that state — so the fight stays mechanically coherent over a long horizon.
That's the practical difference between a world model you can only watch and one you could actually play, where inputs have consequences that persist.
slides.body: Concrete scenario — generating several minutes of continuous combat.
slides.title: Worked Example: A Sustained Boss Fight

rows: Drifts — no ground truth to check against
State acts as an anchor at every step
World model as a game engine: simulate, then render

slides.title: Pixels-Only vs. State-Centric World Models

bullets: Ground-truth state capture is expensive and game-specific — it requires instrumenting a real game engine to log state alongside footage, which doesn't scale to arbitrary web video the way pixels-only training does.
The evaluation is demonstrated on action-RPG combat in one game — it isn't yet shown to generalize to open-world exploration, puzzle mechanics, or non-game interactive video.

Explicit state improves mechanical consistency but adds a harder joint objective: the system has to learn to keep pixels faithful to state, not just plausible-looking, which is more demanding than pixel prediction alone.
slides.title: Where This Approach Is Limited

bullets: Core reframe: an interactive world model should behave like a game engine — simulate authoritative state, then render — not like a camera that only predicts pixels.
State–observation persistence is the missing piece that keeps long rollouts coherent instead of drifting.
It's backed by a purpose-built dataset: 90+ hours of Black Myth: Wukong footage with frame-aligned actions and ground-truth state, from the same group behind the WildWorld dataset.
It was the top trending paper on Hugging Face's Daily Papers for July 19, 2026, with well over 400 upvotes — far ahead of the next most-upvoted paper that week.
slides.title: Takeaways

questions.prompt: What does "state–observation persistence" mean in this paper?
options: Compressing video frames to save storage
Keeping an explicit, checkable game state across the rollout that observations are rendered from
Persisting user preferences between sessions
A caching layer for repeated API calls
questions.explanation: It's the core addition that lets the model act like a game engine: authoritative state is tracked separately from pixels, so long rollouts don't silently drift.

questions.prompt: Why do pixels-only world models tend to drift over long rollouts?
options: They run out of GPU memory
They intentionally randomize outcomes for diversity
They have no explicit state to anchor to — only prior frames, so small errors compound
The training data is too small
questions.explanation: Without ground-truth state as supervision, a model has nothing but its own previous (possibly already-wrong) frames to condition on, so small errors accumulate over a long horizon.

questions.prompt: What data did the authors build to train and evaluate this approach?
options: A synthetic dataset generated entirely by another LLM
A dataset of chess games
Public web video scraped without any state annotations
90+ hours of Black Myth: Wukong gameplay with frame-aligned actions and ground-truth state
questions.explanation: The instrumented gameplay capture is what supplies the ground-truth state signal that pixels-only datasets lack.

references.title: From Pixels to States: Rethinking Interactive World Models as Game Engines (arXiv:2607.14076)
WildWorld dataset (related dataset from the same research group)

posts.title: DeepSeek V4 Goes Stable, Undercuts Frontier Pricing 30x
posts.subtitle: After three months in preview, V4-Pro and V4-Flash ship as open-weight, MIT-licensed models — at a fraction of Opus- and GPT-5.5-class prices.
posts.summary: DeepSeek's V4 family — a 1.6T-parameter MoE model (V4-Pro) and a smaller 284B sibling (V4-Flash) — moved from preview to official stable release in mid-July 2026, shipping as open weights under the MIT license. V4-Pro's output pricing undercuts Claude Opus- and GPT-5.5-class pricing by roughly 30x while reportedly matching Gemini 3.1 Pro on SWE-bench Verified.

slides.body: After nearly three months in preview, DeepSeek's V4 family — Pro and Flash — moved to an official stable release in mid-July 2026, with open weights under the MIT license.
slides.title: DeepSeek V4 Graduates From Preview to Stable

bullets: DeepSeek V4-Pro and V4-Flash first shipped April 24, 2026 as preview models — usable, but without a committed pricing floor or a guarantee that defaults wouldn't change.
Teams building production systems on a preview model take on migration risk: pricing, defaults, or even the weights themselves can shift before a stable release lands.
DeepSeek's older models (deepseek-chat, deepseek-reasoner) were set to be retired from the API by July 24, 2026, adding pressure for a clear, stable successor to move onto.
slides.title: Preview Models Aren't Something You Build On

bullets: V4-Pro: 1.6T total parameters, Mixture-of-Experts with 49B active per token.
V4-Flash: 284B total parameters, 13B active per token — the cheaper, faster sibling.
Both default to a 1M-token context window with up to 384K tokens of output, and both ship as open weights under the MIT license alongside hosted API access.
slides.title: What Actually Shipped

bullets: Mixture-of-Experts routes each token to a small subset of specialist sub-networks instead of running the full model — V4-Pro activates only 49B of its 1.6T parameters per token, roughly 3%.
That selective activation is what makes serving a trillion-parameter-class model at commodity pricing economically viable: you pay compute for the active experts, not the full parameter count.
The tradeoff MoE makes: more total parameters to store (memory/VRAM cost) in exchange for far less compute per token than a dense model of the same total size.
slides.body: A dense 1.6T model would be far too expensive to serve per query.
slides.title: Why a 1.6T Model Is Usable at All

slides.body: On a per-output-token basis, V4-Pro undercuts Opus-class pricing by roughly 28x and GPT-5.5-class pricing by roughly 34x.
slides.title: Pricing vs. Frontier Closed Models

bullets: A workload generating 50M output tokens/month costs roughly $43.50/month on V4-Pro pricing versus roughly $1,250–1,500/month at Opus/GPT-5.5-class pricing — large enough to change what's economically viable to build.
V4-Pro reportedly scores 80.6% on SWE-bench Verified, matching Gemini 3.1 Pro and topping other open-weight models on that benchmark, so the price gap isn't purely a quality tradeoff on coding tasks.
MIT-licensed open weights also mean teams can self-host V4-Flash for latency-sensitive or data-residency-constrained workloads instead of depending on a hosted API at all.
slides.title: Where the Price Gap Actually Matters

bullets: DeepSeek's API uses peak/off-peak pricing — listed prices roughly double during peak hours, so effective cost depends on when traffic actually runs, not just the headline number.

Self-hosting a 1.6T (even MoE) model still requires serious multi-GPU infrastructure to hold the full parameter set in memory, even though inference compute per token is cheap — open weights isn't the same as easy to run yourself.
Cheaper pricing pressures competitors, but headline benchmark parity (e.g. SWE-bench) doesn't guarantee parity on every task — validate on your own eval set before switching.
slides.title: What the Pricing Table Doesn't Show

bullets: DeepSeek V4-Pro (1.6T total / 49B active) and V4-Flash (284B total / 13B active) moved from preview to stable release in mid-July 2026, open-weighted under MIT.
MoE routing is what makes trillion-parameter-class serving affordable: only about 3% of V4-Pro's parameters activate per token.
V4-Pro output pricing undercuts Opus/GPT-5.5-class pricing by roughly 30x while reportedly matching Gemini 3.1 Pro on SWE-bench Verified (80.6%).
Watch for peak/off-peak pricing multipliers and real self-hosting costs before assuming the headline price is what you'll actually pay.
slides.title: Takeaways

questions.prompt: Why can a 1.6T-parameter MoE model like V4-Pro be served far more cheaply than a dense model of the same size?
options: It only activates a small subset of experts (about 49B params) per token
It runs entirely on CPUs
It caches every possible output in advance
It uses a smaller vocabulary
questions.explanation: MoE routes each token to a small set of specialist sub-networks, so compute cost tracks the active parameters (~49B), not the full 1.6T total.

questions.prompt: Roughly how much cheaper is V4-Pro's output pricing than GPT-5.5-class pricing?
options: About 2x cheaper
About the same
Roughly 34x cheaper
V4-Pro is more expensive
questions.explanation: At $0.87/1M output tokens versus roughly $30/1M for GPT-5.5-class pricing, the gap is about 34x.

questions.prompt: What's a real limitation of "open weights, MIT license" for a model like V4-Flash?
options: It can't be used commercially
It's illegal to modify
Running it yourself still requires substantial GPU infrastructure despite the permissive license
Open-weight models cannot use MoE architectures
questions.explanation: A permissive license removes legal friction, not the hardware cost of hosting a large model — you still need enough VRAM to hold the full parameter set.

posts.title: Function Calling: How LLMs Trigger Real Actions
posts.subtitle: The model never runs your code — it proposes a structured call, and your application decides whether to execute it.
posts.summary: Function calling gives a model typed, schema-defined tools instead of free text to describe actions. The model proposes a structured call (name + JSON arguments); your application executes it and feeds the result back in. It's the mechanism underneath nearly every agent framework and protocol, including MCP — but schema conformance doesn't guarantee correct argument values, so validating before executing anything destructive still matters.

slides.body: An LLM can't call your API directly. Function calling is the protocol that lets it describe which function to call and with what arguments, then hands control back to your code to actually run it.
slides.title: Function Calling: How a Model Actually Presses the Button

bullets: Early tool-use hacks asked models to write things like "I should call get_weather(city=Paris)" in plain text, then relied on brittle regex to parse it out of the response.
Free text has no schema: the model might misspell a parameter name, invent an argument that doesn't exist, or wrap the call in extra prose that breaks the parser.
There's also no way to constrain which functions are even valid at a given step, or to enforce argument types — a string where you need an integer silently breaks downstream code.
slides.title: Free-Text Tool Requests Don't Scale

bullets: Each tool is declared once: a name, a natural-language description (this is what the model uses to decide WHEN to call it), and a JSON Schema for its arguments (types, required fields, enums).
Instead of free text, the model emits a structured object — e.g. Anthropic's tool_use content block — naming the function and supplying arguments as valid JSON matching that schema.
The host application, not the model, actually executes the function and returns the result, which is fed back into the conversation as a tool_result before the model continues.
slides.body: Modern function calling gives the model a list of typed function definitions — name, description, and a JSON Schema for parameters — and the model is trained to emit a structured call instead of prose.
slides.title: A Schema the Model Is Trained to Fill In

bullets: 1) You send the user message plus a list of available tool schemas to the model.
2) The model replies with either a normal text answer, or a structured tool call (function name + JSON arguments) instead.
3) Your code executes that function against the real world — an API call, a DB query, a calculator — the model never touches it directly.
4) You send the result back as a tool_result message; the model reads it and either calls another tool or produces a final answer.
slides.title: The Full Round Trip

bullets: Model emits: search_flights(origin="BOS", destination="DEN", date="2026-07-24") — structured and typed, ready to execute without any text parsing.
Your backend runs the real search, gets results, and returns them as a structured tool_result in the next turn.
The model reads the results and either asks a clarifying question ("cheapest or fastest?") or calls book_flight(flight_id=...) next — each step stays a typed call, not prose the app has to re-parse.
slides.body: User: "Find me a flight from Boston to Denver next Friday."
slides.title: Worked Example: Booking a Flight

rows: Schema-validated JSON — fails loudly, not silently
None — everything is a string until you check
Enforced by JSON Schema (types, enums, required fields)
Many APIs support several structured calls in one turn
Explicit: the tool description guides when to call it

slides.title: Function Calling vs. Free-Text Tool Requests

bullets: Schema adherence isn't correctness: a model can still hallucinate a plausible-but-wrong value (e.g. a made-up flight ID) — the schema guarantees shape, not truth, so validate before executing anything destructive.
Too many tools in one request degrades selection accuracy — past roughly a few dozen tool definitions, models start picking the wrong one or missing an obviously relevant one.
Every tool schema costs context tokens on every turn, even unused ones — verbose descriptions and large parameter lists add up fast for agents with many tools.
Not worth the overhead for single-shot Q&A with no external action needed — plain generation is simpler and cheaper.
slides.title: Where Function Calling Still Goes Wrong

bullets: Function calling replaces prose tool-requests with a schema the model is trained to fill in: name plus typed JSON arguments.
The model never executes anything — it only proposes a call; your code runs it and feeds the result back in.

It's the foundation underneath agent frameworks and protocols like MCP, which standardizes how tool schemas are described and discovered — the request/response mechanics underneath are still function calling.
Validate arguments before executing side effects: a schema-valid call can still contain a hallucinated value.
slides.title: Takeaways

questions.prompt: In function calling, who actually executes the chosen function?
options: The model itself, inside its own weights
The host application, after the model proposes a structured call
A separate fine-tuned "executor" model
It's executed automatically with no application involvement
questions.explanation: The model only proposes a name and arguments; your application code is what actually runs the function and returns a result.

questions.prompt: What does a JSON Schema attached to a tool definition actually guarantee?
options: That the argument values are factually correct
That the call's shape — types and required fields — is valid, not that the values are correct
That the function will succeed when executed
That the model will always choose to call this tool
questions.explanation: Schema conformance is a shape guarantee, not a truth guarantee — a model can still fill a valid string field with a hallucinated value.

questions.prompt: What's a known failure mode when a model is given too many tool definitions at once?
options: The API silently drops requests
Function calling stops working entirely
Tool-selection accuracy degrades — the model starts picking the wrong tool or missing relevant ones
The model refuses to respond
questions.explanation: Beyond roughly a few dozen tools, models increasingly mis-select — group or gate tools instead of exposing everything at once.

posts.title: ReAct: The Think-Act-Observe Loop Behind Every Agent
posts.subtitle: Why letting a model reason AND act, one step at a time, beats doing either alone.
posts.summary: ReAct interleaves reasoning traces with tool actions in a single loop — Thought, Action, Observation, repeat — so a model can plan, check its plan against real-world feedback, and replan. It's the pattern underneath nearly every modern LLM agent, from coding assistants to research bots.

slides.title: Two Failure Modes, One Fix
slides.body: Models that only reason hallucinate facts. Models that only act have no plan to recover when something goes wrong. ReAct interleaves both in one loop.

slides.title: Reasoning Alone Drifts From Reality
bullets: Chain-of-thought asks a model to 'think step by step' — but every fact it recalls comes from frozen training data, not the live world.
Ask a CoT-only model a multi-hop factual question and it can reason fluently while confidently citing a wrong number, because nothing forces it to check.
Acting without reasoning has the opposite problem: an agent that just fires off tool calls has no scratchpad to notice it's stuck, looping, or off-plan.
Neither failure mode is rare — they're the default behavior of each approach used alone.

slides.title: Interleave Thought, Action, and Observation
slides.body: ReAct (Yao et al., 2022) generates a Thought, picks an Action from a fixed tool set, receives an Observation from executing it, then loops — feeding that observation back in before the next Thought.
bullets: Thought: free-text reasoning about what's needed next — not shown to any tool, just planning.
Action: a structured call, e.g. Search[query] or Lookup[term], executed outside the model.
Observation: the real result gets appended to the context, grounding the next Thought in what actually happened, not what the model assumed.
The loop repeats until the model emits a Finish[answer] action — reasoning and evidence-gathering happen in the same trace, not two separate passes.

slides.title: Worked Example: A Two-Hop Question
slides.body: "Which mountain range contains the peak that inspired the Paramount Pictures logo, and how tall is it?"
bullets: Thought 1: I need to identify the peak first, then its range and height.
Action 1: Search[Paramount Pictures logo mountain]
Observation 1: The logo is modeled on Artesonraju, a peak in Peru.
Thought 2: Now I need its range and elevation.
Action 2: Search[Artesonraju range elevation]
Observation 2: Cordillera Blanca, Andes; roughly 6,025 m.
Thought 3: I have both facts. Action 3: Finish[Cordillera Blanca (Andes); ~6,025 m]

slides.title: ReAct vs. Reasoning-Only vs. Acting-Only

rows: Yes — thoughts reassess after each observation
Higher — extra thought + observation tokens per step

slides.title: Where ReAct Breaks Down
slides.body: The loop is only as good as the tools and the stopping logic feeding it.
bullets: Repetition traps: if an action returns an unhelpful observation, a model can re-issue the same failing action instead of trying something new — needs explicit loop/step limits.
Noisy observations propagate: a bad search result becomes a bad 'fact' baked into every later Thought, since nothing re-verifies it.
Cost scales with steps: each Thought-Action-Observation round trip adds tokens and a tool round-trip latency — a 10-hop task is far slower and pricier than a single completion.
Not worth it for single-fact lookups or tasks with no useful external tool — plain generation or a single retrieval call is simpler and cheaper.

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: ReAct's core idea: make reasoning and acting share one context, so each grounds the other.
The loop is Thought → Action → Observation → repeat, ending in a Finish action.
It's the ancestor of today's agent frameworks — every 'agent loop' you see in a coding assistant or research tool is a ReAct variant with a bigger tool set.
Guard against runaway loops: cap steps, detect repeated actions, and let the model see enough of the observation to actually revise its plan.

questions.prompt: What does the 'Observation' step in ReAct actually contain?
options: The model's confidence score for its last Thought
The real output returned by executing the chosen Action, fed back into context
A summary the model writes of its own reasoning so far
A human reviewer's approval of the action
questions.explanation: Observation is the actual result of running the Action (e.g., a search result) appended to the context — it's what grounds the next Thought in reality instead of the model's prior assumptions.

questions.prompt: Why does pure chain-of-thought reasoning (no tools) tend to hallucinate on multi-hop factual questions?
options: It uses a smaller model than tool-using agents
It has no mechanism to check facts against anything outside its frozen training data
It doesn't use a system prompt
It always answers in one token
questions.explanation: CoT reasons fluently but every 'fact' it recalls comes from parametric memory — nothing in the loop forces a check against a live source, so confident wrong answers slip through.

questions.prompt: What's a real cost of the ReAct pattern compared to a single-shot completion?
options: It cannot be used with any external tools
It requires fine-tuning the base model before use
Each Thought-Action-Observation round adds tokens and a tool round-trip, so multi-hop tasks are slower and more expensive
It only works with vision models
questions.explanation: Every loop iteration adds a Thought, an Action, and an Observation to the context and requires an external call — a 10-step task costs far more latency and tokens than one completion, so simple lookups shouldn't pay that tax.

references.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models (arXiv)

posts.title: MCP Sampling: When a Tool Server Borrows the Model
posts.subtitle: The MCP primitive that lets a server ask your LLM for a completion — without ever holding an API key.
posts.summary: MCP sampling flips the usual tool-calling direction: instead of an LLM calling a tool, a tool server can ask the client's LLM to generate text on its behalf, while the human stays in the loop to approve or edit the request. It's how MCP servers get real language judgment without shipping their own model subscription or API key.

slides.title: The MCP Primitive Nobody Talks About
slides.body: Tools and Resources let a model read data and take actions. Sampling flips the arrow: a server can ask the client's LLM to think for it — with a human still holding the approve button.

slides.title: A Tool That Needs Judgment Has Two Bad Options
bullets: A git-commit-message server needs to summarize a diff into prose — that's a job for an LLM, not string formatting.
Option A: ship the server with its own model API key. Now every install needs a second subscription and a second bill.
Option B: fall back to crude heuristics instead of real language understanding — the tool gets dumber to avoid the dependency.
Neither option lets the server reuse the model the user already pays for and trusts.

slides.title: Sampling Lets the Server Borrow the Client's Model
slides.body: MCP defines a sampling/createMessage request a server can send back through the client. The client — not the server — decides whether to forward it to an LLM, which model to use, and whether to show the human the prompt first.
bullets: The server never sees an API key or talks to a model provider directly — it only talks to the client over the existing MCP connection.
The request carries messages, an optional systemPrompt, and soft modelPreferences (costPriority, speedPriority, intelligencePriority) instead of naming an exact model.
Sampling is an optional client capability, negotiated per connection — the server must check for it, not assume it.

slides.title: The Round Trip Has a Human Checkpoint Built In
bullets: 1) Server sends sampling/createMessage over the MCP connection, not to any LLM API.
2) The client (the host app, e.g. an IDE or desktop assistant) can surface the proposed prompt to the user for review before doing anything.
3) If approved, the client forwards it to whichever LLM it has configured and gets a completion back.
4) The client may also review the completion before returning it to the server — two human checkpoints, not one.

slides.title: Worked Example: A Commit-Message Tool
slides.body: A local MCP server watches `git diff --staged` and wants to draft a commit message.
bullets: Server sends a sampling request: systemPrompt 'write a conventional commit message', messages containing the diff, modelPreferences={intelligencePriority: 0.3, speedPriority: 0.8} — it wants fast, not brilliant.
The client shows: 'This server wants to generate text using your model. Diff: +42/-11 lines in auth.py. Allow?'
The user approves once (or sets a standing permission); the model drafts 'fix(auth): handle expired refresh tokens'.
The server never touched a model API key — the client's own subscription and model choice paid for the call.

slides.title: Sampling vs. a Server Bringing Its Own Key
slides.body: Sampling trades a little latency and client-side complexity for zero secret sprawl and a human in the loop.

rows: Invisible to the user by default

slides.title: Where Sampling Breaks Down
bullets: It's an optional client capability — many MCP hosts don't implement it, so servers must degrade gracefully rather than assume it exists.
Every hop adds latency: server -> client -> LLM -> client -> server. Not suitable for tight loops needing many completions per second.
modelPreferences are hints, not guarantees — a cost-sensitive server can still get routed to an expensive model if that's all the client has configured.
Overusing approval prompts trains users to click 'allow' reflexively, weakening the human-in-the-loop safeguard sampling was built for.

slides.title: The Takeaway
bullets: Sampling inverts the usual MCP direction: instead of the model calling a tool, a tool asks the model for help.
It removes the API-key burden from tool servers while keeping a human checkpoint on every LLM call a server triggers.
It's a capability, not a guarantee — check for it, degrade without it, and don't spam approval prompts.

questions.prompt: In MCP sampling, which side actually holds the API key and talks to the LLM provider?
options: The tool server
The client/host application
A dedicated sampling proxy
Neither — sampling is keyless end-to-end
questions.explanation: The server never touches an LLM API directly. It sends a sampling/createMessage request over the existing MCP connection, and the client — which already has a model configured and paid for — decides whether and how to fulfill it.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of modelPreferences fields like costPriority and speedPriority in a sampling request?
options: They force the client to use one exact model name
They express soft priorities so the client can pick an appropriate model without the server hardcoding one
They set a hard budget cap the client cannot exceed
They are ignored by the specification and exist only for logging
questions.explanation: Sampling requests avoid naming a specific model. Instead they carry priorities (e.g., prefer cheap/fast over maximally capable) that the client is free to interpret however its configuration allows — the server stays portable across whatever model the user has connected.

questions.prompt: Why does sampling still keep a human in the loop even though it automates an LLM call for a tool?
options: MCP requires cryptographic signing for every request
The client can surface the proposed prompt for approval before forwarding it to an LLM, and can review the completion before returning it
Sampling requests are rate-limited to one per session
The user must manually type every response the server receives
questions.explanation: The client sits between the server and the LLM at two checkpoints — it can show the user what's being asked before sending it, and can review what comes back before handing it to the server. That's what distinguishes sampling from a server silently calling an LLM on its own.

references.title: Model Context Protocol — official specification

posts.title: Hybrid Search: Fusing Keyword and Vector Retrieval
posts.subtitle: Why the best RAG pipelines never trust embeddings alone
posts.summary: Vector search finds meaning but blurs exact terms; BM25 finds exact terms but misses meaning. Hybrid search runs both and merges their rankings with Reciprocal Rank Fusion, a simple formula that beats either retriever alone without needing to normalize incompatible scores.

slides.title: One Query, Two Search Engines
slides.body: Production RAG systems rarely pick embeddings or keyword search — they run both, in parallel, every time.

slides.title: Embeddings Blur the Exact Term You Needed
slides.body: Dense vectors are trained to capture meaning, which means they compress away the precise tokens that rare terms depend on.
bullets: Query 'error code ECONNRESET' — an embedding model may rank a doc about 'connection timeout troubleshooting' above the one page that names ECONNRESET
Product SKUs, part numbers, legal citations, and person names look like noise to a model trained on semantic similarity
BM25 (term-frequency × inverse-document-frequency) nails exact matches instantly but is blind to paraphrase: 'car' and 'automobile' share no tokens

slides.title: Reciprocal Rank Fusion Merges Rankings, Not Scores
slides.body: RRF sidesteps the hardest part of combining retrievers: BM25 scores and cosine similarities live on incompatible scales, so you can't just average them. RRF only looks at each document's rank position.
bullets: RRF(d) = Σ over retrievers of 1 / (k + rank(d)) — sum the reciprocal ranks a document gets from each system
k is a small constant (60 in the original 2009 paper) that dampens the impact of a #1 vs #2 finish so one retriever can't dominate
A document that's rank 3 in BM25 and rank 5 in vector search often outscores one that's rank 1 in only one list

slides.title: Worked Example: Merging Two Top-5 Lists
slides.body: Query: 'impedance mismatch ORM'. BM25 nails the exact phrase; vector search surfaces conceptually related docs it never mentions by name.
bullets: BM25 top result: Doc C (rank 1) — contains the literal phrase 'impedance mismatch'
Vector top result: Doc A (rank 1) — a general 'ORM design patterns' doc with no exact phrase match
Doc B appears rank 2 in BM25 and rank 3 in vector search — it wins the fusion despite never being #1 anywhere

slides.title: Vector-Only vs BM25-Only vs Hybrid
When to Reach for Hybrid Search
bullets: Skip it for small, narrow-domain corpora where one retriever already covers both exact and semantic queries well
Deduplicate documents across the two candidate lists before fusing — otherwise the same doc's two ranks silently double-count
RRF needs no score normalization or trained weights, which is why it's the default fusion method in Elasticsearch, Weaviate, and Vespa
Tune candidate depth (top-k per retriever, typically 20-100) before tuning k — starving either retriever of candidates hurts fusion more than the constant does

questions.prompt: Why does Reciprocal Rank Fusion combine rank positions instead of raw retriever scores?
options: Rank positions are faster to compute than similarity scores
BM25 scores and cosine similarities live on incompatible scales, so averaging them directly is meaningless
Ranks are required by the RAG specification
Scores are not available from vector databases
questions.explanation: A BM25 score of 8.2 and a cosine similarity of 0.71 aren't comparable numbers. Rank position (1st, 2nd, 3rd...) is retriever-agnostic, so RRF can fuse any set of ranked lists without normalization or calibration.

questions.prompt: In RRF(d) = Σ 1/(k + rank(d)), what does the constant k (typically 60) primarily control?
options: The maximum number of documents returned
How much a document's exact position (1st vs 2nd) swings its final score
The embedding dimensionality used by the vector retriever
The number of retrievers that must agree on a document
questions.explanation: A larger k flattens the curve — being rank 1 vs rank 2 matters less to the final score. A small k would let a single #1 finish dominate the fusion, defeating the point of combining multiple retrievers.

questions.prompt: A document ranks #1 in vector search but doesn't appear at all in the BM25 top-k. What happens to it under RRF?
options: It's automatically disqualified for lacking a keyword match
It still gets an RRF score from its vector rank alone, and can still win if other candidates score lower overall
Its score is forced to zero because fusion requires appearing in every list
RRF throws an error and falls back to BM25-only
questions.explanation: RRF sums whatever ranks a document has — missing from one list just means that term contributes 0, not that the document is excluded. This is exactly how hybrid search rescues a strong semantic match that shares no keywords with the query.

references.title: Reciprocal Rank Fusion Outperforms Condorcet and Individual Rank Learning Methods (Cormack, Clarke, Büttcher — SIGIR 2009)

posts.title: PagedAttention: The GPU Memory Trick Behind vLLM
posts.subtitle: How treating the KV cache like OS virtual memory cut serving waste from 60-80% to under 4%
posts.summary: PagedAttention splits each request's KV cache into small fixed-size blocks addressed through a block table, instead of reserving one worst-case contiguous slab per request. That single idea, borrowed from OS virtual memory paging, is what lets vLLM pack far more concurrent requests onto the same GPU and became the default memory-management strategy across modern LLM serving engines.

slides.title: The GPU Memory Trick Behind Every Fast LLM Server
slides.body: PagedAttention is the memory-management technique inside vLLM that turned LLM serving from wasteful, worst-case guesswork into something as disciplined as an OS managing RAM.

slides.title: Naive KV Caching Wastes 60-80% of GPU Memory
bullets: Every generated token needs its key/value vectors cached so future tokens can attend back to it — the KV cache grows with every decoding step of every active request
Early serving systems reserved one long contiguous memory slab per request, sized for the *maximum possible* sequence length, before generation even started

Most requests finish far short of that max — the reserved-but-unused space (internal fragmentation) plus memory carved out but never reused (external fragmentation) can waste 60-80% of allocated KV cache memory
That waste directly caps batch size: fewer concurrent requests fit in GPU memory, so throughput suffers even while the GPU's compute sits idle

slides.title: Treat the KV Cache Like Virtual Memory Pages
slides.body: PagedAttention borrows the classic OS trick: don't allocate memory in one contiguous slab — allocate small fixed-size blocks and let a table point to wherever they physically live.
bullets: Each sequence's KV cache is split into fixed-size blocks (vLLM's default is 16 tokens per block)
Blocks are allocated on demand, one at a time, as generation produces new tokens — no need to guess a max length upfront
A per-sequence 'block table' maps logical block index to physical block location in GPU memory, exactly like a page table maps virtual addresses to physical ones
Because blocks don't need to be contiguous, the allocator packs GPU memory almost perfectly — internal fragmentation is limited to at most one partially-filled block per sequence

slides.title: Bonus: Blocks Can Be Shared, Not Just Packed
bullets: Parallel sampling and beam search often share a common prompt prefix across multiple candidate sequences
Because each block is reference-counted, PagedAttention lets multiple sequences point their block tables at the same physical blocks for that shared prefix
When one branch needs to diverge — write a new token into a shared block — vLLM copies just that one block first: copy-on-write, straight out of OS memory management
This can cut memory use substantially for beam search and parallel sampling, since the shared prompt is stored once instead of once per candidate

slides.title: Worked Example: 3 Requests, One GPU
slides.body: Say a GPU has room for 256 KV-cache blocks of 16 tokens each — 4,096 tokens of total capacity.
bullets: Naive allocator: reserves a 2,048-token contiguous slab per request 'just in case' — only 2 requests fit, even if both actually finish after 100 tokens
PagedAttention: request A gets blocks as it generates — after 100 tokens it holds 7 blocks (112 tokens of capacity), not 128 reserved-but-empty blocks
Blocks freed by a request that finishes early become available immediately, so a 3rd or 4th request can start as soon as blocks free up — no waiting on one giant reserved slab to close out
Net effect: far more concurrent sequences fit in the same physical memory, which is what actually drives serving throughput

slides.title: The Headline Numbers
slides.body: vLLM's own reporting on the naive-vs-paged comparison for KV cache memory:

slides.title: Where Paging Costs You Something
bullets: Non-contiguous blocks mean the attention kernel must gather scattered memory instead of reading one linear array — vLLM ships custom CUDA kernels specifically to make that gather fast, it isn't free on arbitrary hardware
Block size is a tuning knob: smaller blocks reduce internal fragmentation but add more block-table bookkeeping overhead per token processed
For workloads with short, uniform-length outputs and small batch sizes, naive allocation wastes little anyway — paging's win shrinks when there's little fragmentation to reclaim
Copy-on-write sharing specifically helps beam search and parallel sampling; it does nothing for unrelated concurrent requests that share no prefix

slides.title: Why This Became the Default
bullets: PagedAttention turned KV cache memory from a hard-coded worst-case reservation into an on-demand, near-fully-packed resource
It's the core idea inside vLLM, now one of the most widely deployed open-source LLM inference engines

The lesson generalizes: LLM serving increasingly borrows decades-old OS techniques — paging, copy-on-write, scheduling — because GPU memory pressure looks a lot like the RAM problem operating systems solved long ago
Pair it with continuous batching (scheduling) and you get the two pillars most modern LLM serving stacks are built on

questions.prompt: What specific problem does PagedAttention solve in LLM serving?
options: It reduces the number of floating-point operations in the attention computation
It eliminates KV cache memory waste caused by reserving contiguous max-length blocks upfront
It makes tokenization faster
It removes the need for a KV cache entirely
questions.explanation: PagedAttention doesn't change attention's math — it changes how the resulting KV cache is stored in memory, replacing wasteful worst-case contiguous pre-allocation with on-demand fixed-size blocks.

questions.prompt: In vLLM, what maps a sequence's logical KV cache blocks to their physical location in GPU memory?
options: The tokenizer vocabulary
A block table, analogous to an OS page table
The attention mask
The model's positional embeddings
questions.explanation: The block table is the indirection layer that lets each sequence's blocks live scattered anywhere in physical GPU memory while still being addressed in logical order — exactly like a page table in virtual memory.

questions.prompt: Why can beam search or parallel sampling save memory under PagedAttention?
options: Beam search automatically switches to a smaller model
Candidate sequences sharing a prompt prefix can share the same physical blocks via copy-on-write
PagedAttention disables the extra candidate branches
The KV cache is skipped entirely for beam search
questions.explanation: Because blocks are reference-counted, multiple sequences can point to the same physical block for a shared prefix; only when a branch writes a new, diverging token does vLLM copy that one block — copy-on-write.

posts.title: BadWAM: When Robot Brains Dream Right, Act Wrong
posts.subtitle: A new attack proves a robot's imagined future can look perfect while its hands do something else
posts.summary: BadWAM shows that World-Action Models — networks that jointly imagine a robot's future and choose its next action — can be attacked so the imagined future stays believable while the executed action silently drifts. An action-only attack collapses task success from 96.5% to 43.1%, and a stealthier variant preserves the model's predicted future almost unchanged while still hijacking the action.

slides.title: A Robot Can Imagine the Right Future and Still Do the Wrong Thing
slides.body: BadWAM (Li, Yang & Wang, NUS) introduces World-Action Drift Attacks: tiny pixel perturbations that break the link between what a robot's model predicts will happen and what it actually commands the robot to do.

slides.title: One Network, Two Jobs, One Unchecked Assumption
bullets: World-Action Models (WAMs) fuse two jobs into one network: predict the future frame an action produces, and choose that action

Three common WAM designs tested: Direct (action straight from observation), Joint (action and imagined future predicted together), and IDM (Inverse Dynamics Model, action inferred from a predicted future)
Robotics teams have started trusting 'if the imagined future looks right, the action must be right' — that link was never adversarially tested
BadWAM is the first paper to specifically target the imagination-to-action alignment inside a WAM, not just its final output

slides.title: Two Attacks: One Loud, One Silent
slides.body: Both attacks add a small ℓ∞-bounded pixel perturbation (bound 0.06) to the camera input, within a 16-query attack budget — nothing a human watching the video feed would notice.
bullets: Action-only attack: perturbs the observation purely to maximize how far the commanded action shifts from the clean action — fast and effective, doesn't care what the model 'thinks' will happen
Imagination-preserving attack: perturbs the observation to shift the action while keeping the model's predicted future frame close to its clean (unattacked) prediction
That second attack is the dangerous one: an operator monitoring the model's imagined rollout sees a normal, plausible future — while the robot's real action has already been hijacked
Attacks are evaluated in patch or full-image (full_linf) perturbation modes, tested against Direct, Joint, and IDM model variants

slides.title: Worked Example: A Manipulation Task Goes Quietly Wrong
slides.body: In LIBERO, a robot arm is instructed to pick up an object it can see clearly on a table.
bullets: Clean run: camera feed → WAM imagines the gripper closing on the object → arm executes that action → task succeeds
Attacked run (imagination-preserving): attacker adds an imperceptible perturbation to the camera frame
The WAM's predicted future frame still shows the gripper correctly closing on the object — indistinguishable from the clean prediction
But the action actually sent to the arm has drifted just enough that the gripper closes on empty air — the dashboard 'looks fine,' the task fails

slides.title: The Numbers: A Robot That Looks Unshaken
slides.body: Evaluated across LIBERO (closed-loop manipulation) and RoboTwin, the action-only attack alone is enough to gut task performance.

rows: Action drifts; predicted future stays visually close to clean

slides.title: Why This Is a Blind Spot, Not Just a New CVE
bullets: Most robot-safety review today watches the model's imagined rollout as a proxy for whether the action is safe — BadWAM shows that proxy can be actively fooled
It only requires camera-level perturbation, not any access to the model's weights, controller, or action head
The attack transfers across model designs (Direct, Joint, IDM), so it isn't a quirk of one architecture
Not a reason to panic about every deployed robot — LIBERO/RoboTwin are controlled benchmarks, and real deployments add sensor fusion and physical guards the paper doesn't attack

slides.title: What Defenders Should Take From This
bullets: Don't certify a WAM's safety by eyeballing its imagined future alone — the two can be deliberately decoupled
Cross-check the executed action against the imagined future with an independent consistency check, not the same network that generated both
Code is open-sourced (MIT license) specifically so red teams can reproduce and probe further
This is a template for a whole new class of embodied-AI security testing: attack the seam between perception, imagination, and control, not just the final classifier output

questions.prompt: What does the 'imagination-preserving' attack specifically try to keep unchanged?
options: The robot's battery level
The model's predicted future frame, even as it shifts the executed action
The camera's resolution
The training dataset
questions.explanation: That's the stealthy part of BadWAM: the attack keeps the WAM's imagined future close to what it would predict without the attack, so a human or dashboard watching the 'imagination' sees nothing wrong, while the actual commanded action has drifted.

questions.prompt: Roughly what happened to task success rate under the action-only attack in BadWAM's evaluation?
options: It stayed at 96.5%
It rose slightly to 99%
It collapsed from about 96.5% to about 43.1%
It dropped to exactly 0%
questions.explanation: The action-only attack — which doesn't even try to preserve a plausible imagined future — was enough to cut task success from 96.5% down to roughly 43.1%, showing how fragile the action head can be to small, targeted perturbations.

questions.prompt: Why is BadWAM a different kind of threat model than a typical adversarial attack on an image classifier?
options: It requires physical access to the robot's motors
It only works on text models
It targets the alignment between a model's internal imagined future and its externally executed action, not just a single output label
It needs the attacker to retrain the model first
questions.explanation: Classic adversarial attacks push a classifier's output label across a decision boundary. BadWAM instead exploits that a World-Action Model produces two correlated outputs — a predicted future and an action — and shows those two outputs can be deliberately pulled apart.

references.title: BadWAM: When World-Action Models Dream Right but Act Wrong (arXiv:2607.15207)
BadWAM official code repository

posts.title: Robostral Navigate: One Camera Is Enough to Steer a Robot
posts.subtitle: Mistral's first robotics model beats multi-sensor rivals using nothing but a single RGB feed
posts.summary: Mistral AI shipped Robostral Navigate, an 8B-parameter model that lets wheeled, legged, and flying robots navigate unfamiliar spaces from a single RGB camera and plain-language instructions. Trained entirely in simulation on ~400,000 paths across ~6,000 virtual environments, it beats both single-camera and multi-sensor baselines on the R2R-CE benchmark — but ships as an enterprise-only, proprietary model, not open weights.

slides.title: Mistral's First Robot Model Skips the Sensor Stack
slides.body: Robostral Navigate steers wheeled, legged, and flying robots through spaces they've never seen, using one RGB camera and a plain-language instruction — no depth sensor, no LIDAR, no multi-camera rig.

slides.title: Robot Navigation Usually Means Expensive Hardware
bullets: Most vision-language navigation systems lean on depth sensors, LIDAR, or multiple cameras to build a reliable 3D understanding of the space
That hardware adds cost, weight, and calibration overhead — a real constraint for cheaper wheeled robots or small drones
Sim-to-real transfer is notoriously hard: a policy trained in simulation often falls apart on real camera noise, lighting, and clutter
The open question: can a robot navigate purely from what a single, ordinary camera sees, and still hold up against sensor-rich baselines?

slides.title: An 8B Model, Trained Entirely in Simulation
slides.body: Robostral Navigate is an 8B-parameter model built in-house by Mistral, taking a single RGB stream plus a language instruction and outputting navigation commands.
bullets: Training data: roughly 400,000 recorded navigation paths across about 6,000 distinct simulated environments — no real-world data used in training
Works across robot embodiments: wheeled, legged, and flying platforms all steer from the same model
Commands are plain language ('go to the kitchen and stop near the fridge'), not coordinates or waypoints
The bet: a model that never sees real pixels during training can still generalize to real cameras at deployment, purely from monocular RGB

slides.title: Worked Example: Navigating a Space It's Never Seen
slides.body: Drop the robot into an unfamiliar building and give it a plain-language goal.
bullets: Instruction: 'go to the kitchen and stop near the fridge'
The model reads the live monocular RGB stream frame by frame, builds an implicit sense of layout and progress toward the goal, and issues movement commands
No pre-built map, no depth channel, no waypoint list — just camera plus language, continuously, until the goal is reached or the episode ends
This is evaluated on R2R-CE, a continuous 3D navigation benchmark (harder than the original graph-based Room-to-Room task, since the robot moves freely rather than hopping between fixed viewpoints)

slides.title: Beating Multi-Sensor Rigs With Fewer Sensors
slides.body: On R2R-CE validation-unseen, Mistral reports Robostral Navigate at 76.6% success — 9.7 points above the best single-camera system and 4.5 points above the best system using depth or multiple cameras. On validation-seen it reaches 79.4%.

slides.title: The Catch: Proprietary, Simulation-Only Training
bullets: Unlike most of Mistral's language models, Robostral Navigate ships under a proprietary license — enterprise access only, no open weights on release
Trained purely on simulated environments, so the real-world sim-to-real gap in cluttered, dynamic, or poorly-lit spaces is still an open question outside Mistral's own reporting
A single RGB camera has no direct depth signal, which is a real failure mode in low-light, glass, or texture-less environments where depth sensors would normally help

When not to reach for it: safety-critical navigation (warehouses with fast-moving forklifts, outdoor autonomous delivery) where certified redundant depth sensing is a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have

slides.title: Why This Release Matters
bullets: It's Mistral's first robotics model — a new front for a company best known for open-weight LLMs
It's a real engineering result: beating sensor-rich baselines with less hardware lowers the cost floor for deploying navigating robots
It reinforces a 2026 trend — foundation-model labs moving from chat models into physical/embodied AI
Enterprise-only access means most developers will only get to evaluate it through Mistral's hosted offering, not by downloading weights

questions.prompt: What's unusual about Robostral Navigate's sensor requirements compared to typical robot navigation systems?
options: It requires LIDAR plus stereo cameras
It navigates using only a single RGB camera, no depth sensor or multi-camera rig
It needs a pre-built 3D map of every environment
It only works indoors with fixed cameras
questions.explanation: Most vision-language navigation systems lean on depth sensors or multiple cameras. Robostral Navigate is trained to navigate from a single monocular RGB stream, which is the whole point of the release — it beats sensor-richer baselines with less hardware.

questions.prompt: Where did Robostral Navigate's training data come from?
options: Real-world robot footage collected by Mistral's field team
Roughly 400,000 recorded paths across about 6,000 simulated environments, with no real-world training data
User-submitted videos from the Le Chat app
A single very large real-world warehouse dataset
questions.explanation: Robostral Navigate was trained entirely in simulation — about 400,000 recorded navigation paths across roughly 6,000 distinct virtual spaces — betting on sim-to-real transfer rather than real-world data collection.

questions.prompt: Is Robostral Navigate available as open weights, the way many of Mistral's language models are?
options: Yes, it's fully open-weight under Apache 2.0
No — at launch it's a proprietary model available only through enterprise access
It's open-weight but only for academic researchers
It's open-weight but requires a paid HuggingFace subscription
questions.explanation: Unlike much of Mistral's LLM lineup, Robostral Navigate shipped as a proprietary, enterprise-access model — a notable departure from the open-weight release pattern the company is best known for.
