posts.title: Event-Driven Architecture: Stop Calling, Start Announcing
posts.subtitle: Why services that publish facts instead of making requests scale, fail, and deploy differently
posts.summary: Event-driven architecture replaces direct service-to-service calls with published events that any number of consumers can react to independently. It trades immediate consistency and simple debugging for loose coupling, failure isolation, and independent scaling — but only pays off once you accept eventual consistency, idempotent consumers, and distributed tracing as the cost of admission.

slides.title: What If Services Never Called Each Other Directly?
slides.body: Event-driven architecture (EDA) has producers publish facts about what happened, and lets any number of consumers react — without the producer ever knowing who's listening.

slides.title: Synchronous Calls Create Hidden Coupling
slides.body: A checkout API that calls inventory, payment, shipping, and notification services directly, one after another, looks simple — until one of them is slow.
bullets: Temporal coupling: every downstream service must be up and responsive right now, or the whole request fails or blocks
Cascading failures: a slow payment service backs up threads in the checkout service, which backs up its callers, and so on up the chain
Deployment coupling: changing inventory's API shape means coordinating a release with every service that calls it directly
The caller pays the full latency of every downstream call, even ones whose result it doesn't need before responding to the user

slides.title: Publish Facts, Let Consumers Decide
slides.body: Instead of calling inventory/payment/shipping directly, the checkout service publishes one event — OrderPlaced — to a broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SNS/SQS, EventBridge) and returns immediately.
bullets: Producers don't know or care who consumes an event, or how many consumers there are
Consumers subscribe independently and can be added or removed without touching the producer at all
The checkout API can acknowledge the order the instant the event is durably published — it doesn't wait for inventory, payment, or shipping to finish
New functionality (fraud scoring, analytics, a loyalty-points service) can subscribe to the same event stream without a single line of checkout code changing

slides.title: Three Flavors of 'Event', Not One
bullets: Event Notification: a thin event ('OrderPlaced, id=123') — consumers call back to fetch details they need. Small events, but adds a network round-trip and a dependency on the producer's API staying up
Event-Carried State Transfer: the event carries the full payload (order id, items, totals) — no callback needed, but the schema now has to be versioned carefully as it grows
Event Sourcing: the event log itself is the source of truth; current state is derived by replaying events, not stored separately — powerful for audit trails, but a bigger architectural commitment
Most systems use event-carried state transfer for everyday integration and reach for full event sourcing only where an audit trail or time-travel debugging genuinely pays for its complexity

slides.title: Worked Example: One Event, Three Independent Reactions
slides.body: Checkout publishes a single OrderPlaced event with orderId, userId, and items.
bullets: Inventory service consumes it and reserves stock — if it's briefly down, the event waits in the broker and gets processed when it's back, instead of failing the checkout
Payment service consumes the same event and charges the card, publishing its own PaymentCharged event when done
Notification service consumes OrderPlaced independently and emails a receipt — it doesn't need to know payment succeeded first
None of these three services call each other; each only knows about events, which is what makes it safe to add a fourth consumer later with zero coordination

slides.title: Synchronous REST vs. Event-Driven

rows: A slow/down callee blocks the caller
A down consumer just falls behind, doesn't block the producer
Requires distributed tracing across async hops

slides.title: The Costs Nobody Mentions in the Diagram
bullets: Eventual consistency confuses users: 'my order shows placed' but inventory hasn't reserved stock yet — UX has to account for that lag
Most brokers guarantee at-least-once delivery, not exactly-once — consumers must be idempotent (dedupe by event ID) or they'll double-charge, double-ship, or double-email
Event schemas evolve; a field renamed without versioning silently breaks every consumer that reads it

When NOT to use it: a 2-3 service CRUD app with no real scaling or failure-isolation need, or any interaction where the caller genuinely needs a synchronous yes/no answer right now (e.g., 'is this card valid') before it can proceed

slides.title: The Real Trade You're Making
bullets: EDA buys independent scaling, deployment, and failure isolation — it costs you immediate consistency and a simple call stack
Keep events small and versioned; treat the event schema like a public API contract, because it is one
Make every consumer idempotent by default — 'at-least-once delivery' is the norm, not the exception
Pick sync vs. async per interaction, not architecture-wide — the best systems mix both deliberately

questions.prompt: In event-driven architecture, who decides which services react to a given event?
options: The producer, which maintains a list of authorized consumers
A central orchestrator service that routes every event manually
The consumers themselves — they subscribe independently, and the producer doesn't know who's listening
The database administrator, via stored procedures
questions.explanation: The core of the decoupling is that producers publish events without any knowledge of who consumes them. Consumers opt in by subscribing, which means new consumers can be added with zero changes to the producer.

questions.prompt: Most message brokers guarantee 'at-least-once' delivery. What problem does that create, and how do you fix it?
options: Messages can be lost entirely; fix by polling more frequently
Consumers may receive the same event more than once; fix by making consumers idempotent, e.g. deduping by event ID
Events arrive out of order across all topics; fix by using synchronous calls instead
The broker becomes a single point of failure; fix by removing the broker entirely
questions.explanation: At-least-once delivery means a consumer might see the same event twice (after a retry, a rebalance, etc.). The standard fix is idempotent processing — tracking which event IDs have already been handled — so a duplicate delivery doesn't double-charge or double-ship.

questions.prompt: What's the key difference between Event Notification and Event-Carried State Transfer?
options: Event Notification is only used in event sourcing, Event-Carried State Transfer never is
Event Notification sends a thin 'something happened' event requiring a callback for details; Event-Carried State Transfer includes the full payload so no callback is needed
They are two names for the exact same pattern
Event-Carried State Transfer can only be used with synchronous REST
questions.explanation: Event Notification keeps events small but forces consumers to call back to the producer's API for details, re-adding a runtime dependency. Event-Carried State Transfer avoids that callback by putting the needed data directly in the event, at the cost of a larger, more carefully versioned payload.

references.title: What do you mean by "Event-Driven"? — Martin Fowler

posts.title: SearchOS: Search Agents Get an Operating System
posts.subtitle: Treating open-domain research like process scheduling, not chat
posts.summary: SearchOS-V1 reframes open-domain information seeking as filling out a structured table with cited evidence instead of stuffing findings into a chat transcript. By giving agents a shared Frontier Memory, Evidence Graph, Coverage Map, and Strategy Memory, it leads every baseline on the WideSearch and GISA benchmarks, especially on tasks that need broad, verifiable enumeration.

slides.title: Search Agents Keep Losing the Plot
slides.body: SearchOS-V1 gives multi-agent search systems something they've never had: durable, shared memory of what's been found, what's missing, and what already failed.

slides.title: Chat History Is a Terrible Database
bullets: Single- and multi-agent search systems track progress implicitly, buried in a growing conversation transcript
As the history grows, agents lose track of what they already tried and repeat failed queries
When a search comes up empty, systems get stuck in retry loops that burn the search budget
The end result: incomplete tables, missing entities, and unverifiable answers on broad research questions

slides.title: Reframe the Task as Filling a Table
slides.body: SearchOS treats open-domain information seeking as relational schema completion: discover entities, populate their attributes across linked tables, and anchor every value to a source.
bullets: Search-Oriented Context Management (SOCM) externalizes state into four shared structures instead of conversation history
Frontier Memory: a task queue with a dependency DAG tracking what's blocked, what's ready, and what's done
Evidence Graph: every finding stored with its source and a confidence score, plus edges marking support or conflict
Coverage Map: an entity x attribute schema with per-column type rules, so the system always knows what cell is still empty

slides.title: Four Agent Roles, One Shared State
bullets: Orchestrator: the sole decision-maker, dispatching empty cells from the Coverage Map to sub-agents
Explore agent: handles query typing and entity discovery before the real search begins
Search agents: run in pipelined parallel, each retrieving evidence for one assigned cell
A Search Tool Middleware Harness auto-extracts evidence into the graph after every page retrieval, and a Strategy Memory logs which methods already failed so agents stop repeating them

slides.title: It Wins Hardest on Broad Enumeration
slides.body: Across two open-domain benchmarks, SearchOS leads every F1 metric against five strong single- and multi-agent baselines — with the largest gap on tasks that require recalling many items, not just one fact.

slides.title: Worked Example: 'Every AI Lab's Flagship Model in 2026'
slides.body: Ask a plain ReAct agent this and it drifts: it finds five labs, forgets to check a sixth, and can't tell you which answers it's actually sure of.
bullets: SearchOS first builds a Coverage Map: rows = AI labs, columns = model name, release date, parameter count, source
The Orchestrator dispatches each empty cell to a Search agent instead of one agent chasing the whole question
Each finding lands in the Evidence Graph tagged with its source URL and a confidence score — no source, no cell fill
The Frontier Memory tracks which labs are still unresolved, so the run ends only when the table is actually complete

slides.title: Why This Matters Beyond One Benchmark
bullets: Separating search STATE from conversation HISTORY is the real contribution — it's what makes long research runs reproducible
Grounding every cell to a citation turns 'the agent said so' into 'the agent can show you where'
The biggest wins show up on enumeration-heavy tasks (compile all X), not single-fact lookups
This is a template other agentic-RAG systems can borrow even without adopting the full SearchOS stack

slides.title: When Not to Reach for This
bullets: For a single, narrow factual question, the Coverage Map / Evidence Graph overhead is pure latency with no payoff
The framework assumes a schema (rows/columns) exists or can be inferred — open-ended exploratory research fits less cleanly
Maintaining a Strategy Memory of failed approaches only pays off on long, multi-step runs, not quick lookups

questions.prompt: What's the core reframing SearchOS applies to open-domain information seeking?
options: Treating it as a single long chat conversation with more retries
Treating it as relational schema completion with grounded citations
Replacing all search agents with one large reasoning model
Skipping retrieval entirely and relying on parametric memory
questions.explanation: SearchOS models the task as populating an entity x attribute table (a schema), where every cell must be filled with source-anchored evidence — not as an open-ended chat.

questions.prompt: Where does SearchOS store the fact that a particular search strategy already failed?
options: Evidence Graph
Coverage Map
Strategy Memory
Frontier Memory
questions.explanation: Strategy Memory specifically tracks methodology failures and budget consumption, so agents don't repeat approaches that already didn't work.

questions.prompt: On which kind of benchmark did SearchOS show its largest advantage over baselines like ReAct and Plan-and-Solve?
options: Single-fact lookup questions
Broad enumeration tasks requiring many linked items (GISA Set F1)
Code generation benchmarks
Image classification tasks
questions.explanation: SearchOS's GISA Set F1 score beat the best baseline by +13.4 points — its structured, parallel-dispatch approach pays off most when a task requires recalling and verifying many items, not just one.

references.title: SearchOS-V1: Towards Robust Open-Domain Information-Seeking Agent Collaboration (arXiv:2607.15257)

posts.title: RoPE: Teaching Attention Where Tokens Sit
posts.subtitle: Rotate, don't add — how modern LLMs encode position
posts.summary: Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) encode a token's position by rotating its query and key vectors instead of adding a position vector to the input. Because a rotation's effect on the attention dot product depends only on the RELATIVE angle between two tokens, RoPE bakes relative position awareness directly into attention — and it's the default in LLaMA, Qwen, DeepSeek, Mistral, and most open frontier models.

slides.title: How Does a Transformer Know Word Order?
slides.body: Self-attention on its own is permutation-invariant — shuffle the tokens and you get the same output. Every transformer needs some way to inject position. RoPE is the mechanism nearly every modern open LLM picked.

slides.title: Why Older Position Schemes Broke Down
bullets: Learned absolute position embeddings (original GPT/BERT-style) are a lookup table sized to a max length — they simply don't exist for position 8,193 if trained on 8,192
Adding a position vector to token embeddings only weakly threads through many attention layers
Absolute schemes encode 'token 5 is at position 5', not 'token 5 is 3 places before token 8' — but attention really cares about RELATIVE distance
Models trained this way extrapolate badly past their training context length

slides.title: Rotate the Vector Instead of Adding to It
slides.body: RoPE splits each query/key vector into 2D pairs of dimensions and rotates each pair by an angle proportional to the token's position, using a different rotation frequency per pair.
bullets: For position m, dimension pair i, the rotation angle is m * theta_i, where theta_i = base^(-2i/d) — smaller i rotates fast, larger i rotates slow
Because rotating query by angle m and key by angle n leaves their dot product dependent only on (m - n), relative position falls straight out of the attention math
No extra parameters, no lookup table — position is applied at attention time, layer by layer
The low-frequency pairs (large i) behave almost like absolute position over long distances; high-frequency pairs capture fine-grained local order

slides.title: Worked Example: Rotating a 2D Pair
slides.body: Take one dimension-pair (x, y) of a key vector at position m=3, with rotation angle theta=0.5 rad for that pair.
bullets: Rotation angle for this token: m * theta = 3 * 0.5 = 1.5 radians
New pair: (x*cos(1.5) - y*sin(1.5), x*sin(1.5) + y*cos(1.5)) — same length, just spun around the origin
A token at position m=7 with the same theta rotates by 3.5 radians — the ANGULAR GAP between position 3 and 7 is fixed at 2.0 radians regardless of where they sit in a longer sequence
That fixed angular gap is exactly what makes the dot product between any two tokens depend only on their distance apart

slides.title: RoPE vs. the Alternatives

rows: Weak by default, strong with scaling tricks

slides.title: Where RoPE Still Falls Short
bullets: Vanilla RoPE degrades noticeably once inference sequences run past the training context length — the model has never seen those large rotation angles
Fixing this needs extra tricks at inference or fine-tuning time: Position Interpolation (compress positions to fit), NTK-aware scaling (adjust theta's base), or YaRN (blend both per-frequency)
Choice of the 'base' constant (commonly 10,000, sometimes raised to 500,000+ for long-context models) changes how far the model can extrapolate before breaking down
None of this is free — context extension techniques trade off some short-context precision for long-context reach

slides.title: The Core Idea to Keep
bullets: RoPE encodes position as a rotation, not an addition — and rotations compose, so relative position emerges from the attention dot product for free
It needs zero extra learned parameters, unlike absolute embedding tables
It's why you can even talk about 'extending' an LLM's context window after training — you're really retuning the rotation frequencies
If you're reading a modern open-weight model's config and see 'rope_theta', that's this base constant controlling exactly this tradeoff

questions.prompt: What does RoPE do to a query or key vector to encode its position?
options: Adds a learned position vector to it
Rotates pairs of its dimensions by an angle proportional to position
Concatenates a one-hot position index to it
Multiplies it by the sequence length
questions.explanation: RoPE splits the vector into 2D pairs and rotates each pair by an angle that scales with the token's position and a per-pair frequency — no extra parameters needed.

questions.prompt: Why does rotating queries and keys make attention depend on RELATIVE position?
options: Because rotation angles are randomly initialized
Because the dot product of two rotated vectors depends only on the difference between their rotation angles
Because rotation removes position information entirely
Because keys are rotated but queries are not
questions.explanation: Rotating query by angle m and key by angle n changes their dot product in a way that depends only on (m - n) — the relative offset — not on m and n individually.

questions.prompt: What problem do techniques like YaRN and NTK-aware scaling address?
options: Reducing the number of attention heads
Vanilla RoPE degrading on sequences longer than the model saw during training
Speeding up tokenization
Removing the need for a KV cache
questions.explanation: Vanilla RoPE hasn't seen the large rotation angles that show up past its training length, so it degrades there. YaRN and NTK-aware scaling adjust the rotation frequencies so the model extrapolates better.

posts.title: SEED Trains Agents by Having Them Coach Their Past Selves
posts.subtitle: A self-evolving analyzer keeps hindsight critique in sync with the policy it's training — no separate model needed at inference.
posts.summary: SEED (Self-Evolving On-Policy Distillation), the #2 trending paper on Hugging Face Daily Papers this week, trains long-horizon LLM agents by having one policy checkpoint both act and critique its own trajectories. Unlike prior methods that freeze the critiquing 'analyzer' at the start of training, SEED refreshes it every RL update so hindsight supervision never goes stale — and only a single policy needs to be served at inference.

slides.title: SEED Trains Agents by Having Them Coach Their Past Selves
slides.body: SEED (Self-Evolving On-Policy Distillation for Agentic Reinforcement Learning) is a training recipe for long-horizon LLM agents, published July 16, 2026.
bullets: #2 trending paper on Hugging Face Daily Papers the week of July 17, 2026
From Jinyang Wu, Shuo Yang, Zhengxi Lu and collaborators at Tsinghua University and Tongji University
Targets agents trained with reinforcement learning on long-horizon tasks: ALFWorld, WebShop, and search-based QA

slides.title: A Frozen Critic Grades Yesterday's Mistakes
slides.body: Hindsight-skill methods reflect on a finished trajectory and extract what the agent should have done differently — but that signal only helps if the critique matches what the current policy still gets wrong.
bullets: A one-time, frozen 'analyzer' trained at the start of RL keeps evaluating trajectories against the policy's old behavior, so its supervision drifts stale as the policy improves
Extracting hindsight skills as a live, separate reasoning step at inference means running (and serving) two models instead of one
Static distillation baselines plateau because the teacher signal stops tracking the student's evolving failure modes

slides.title: One Checkpoint, Two Roles, Refreshed Every Round
slides.body: SEED's core move: the same policy plays both actor and analyzer, and the analyzer role is re-synced from the latest policy after every update.
bullets: Stage 1 — hindsight-skill SFT: teach the base model to review a completed trajectory and articulate the skill it should have applied

Stage 2 — self-evolving on-policy distillation: during RL, the current policy acts to collect trajectories, then the same (frozen-for-this-step) policy analyzes them into hindsight skills, which are distilled back into the policy being trained
After each RL update, the newly improved policy becomes next round's analyzer — hindsight supervision evolves in lockstep with the policy instead of lagging behind it
Only the plain policy is needed at inference; no separate analyzer call per action

slides.title: Three Ways to Give an Agent Hindsight

rows: Frozen at the start — drifts from the policy
Refreshed every RL update — matches current policy
Cheap (single policy; skills folded in during training)

slides.title: A Household Task Gone Wrong, Then Corrected
slides.body: Consider an ALFWorld episode: 'heat an apple, then put it in the fridge.' The agent opens the fridge before the apple is actually heated and stalls.
bullets: In hindsight-skill SFT, the model learns to review that trajectory and state the missing step in plain language: 'check the microwave finished before opening the fridge'
During RL, the current policy generates fresh attempts at similar tasks; the synchronized analyzer critiques only mistakes the policy is still actually making right now
That skill gets distilled into the acting policy's own action distribution — so on the next similar task, the correct ordering shows up as instinct, not as an extra reasoning step at inference

slides.title: Where This Recipe Strains
slides.body: SEED is a training-time recipe, and that has real costs and limits.
bullets: Needs goal-directed, multi-step tasks where 'what should I have done differently' is a meaningful question — less useful for single-turn Q&A with no trajectory to critique
Every RL step now also runs a self-critique pass before distillation, adding training-time compute on top of ordinary rollout-and-update cost
Distillation can only transfer skills the base model already has the capacity to represent — hindsight critique alone can't inject knowledge or abilities the model fundamentally lacks

slides.title: The Recipe in Four Lines
bullets: Two stages: hindsight-skill SFT teaches the model to critique, then self-evolving on-policy distillation folds critiques back in during RL
The fix over prior static-distillation baselines is refreshing the analyzer every round instead of freezing it
Ships as a single inference-time policy — no separate analyzer model to serve
Evaluated across three different long-horizon agent settings: ALFWorld, WebShop, and search-based QA

questions.prompt: What specific problem does SEED's self-evolving analyzer solve that a frozen/static analyzer doesn't?
options: It reduces the number of parameters in the model
It keeps the hindsight critique aligned with the policy's current failure modes instead of grading against outdated behavior
It removes the need for reinforcement learning entirely
It only works for vision-language models
questions.explanation: A frozen analyzer trained once at the start keeps evaluating trajectories against the policy's past behavior; refreshing the analyzer every round keeps the critique matched to what the current policy still gets wrong.

questions.prompt: How many models does SEED require at inference time?
options: Two — one policy and one separate analyzer
One — the same policy that was trained, with skills already distilled in
Three — actor, critic, and reward model
It depends on task difficulty
questions.explanation: Because the hindsight skill is distilled back into the acting policy during training, only a single model is needed to deploy — no separate analyzer call per step.

questions.prompt: On which types of benchmarks was SEED evaluated?
options: Image classification benchmarks like ImageNet
Long-horizon, multi-step agent tasks: ALFWorld, WebShop, and search-based QA
Only chatbot conversation benchmarks
Speech recognition benchmarks
questions.explanation: SEED targets long-horizon LLM agents, so it's evaluated on embodied/interactive tasks (ALFWorld household tasks, WebShop shopping tasks) and search-based QA rather than static single-turn benchmarks.

references.title: SEED: Self-Evolving On-Policy Distillation for Agentic Reinforcement Learning (arXiv)

posts.title: GPT-Live Ends the Walkie-Talkie Era of Voice AI
posts.subtitle: OpenAI's full-duplex voice model listens and speaks at once, and quietly hands off hard questions to a frontier model mid-conversation.
posts.summary: OpenAI launched GPT-Live on July 8, 2026: a full-duplex voice model for ChatGPT that can listen and speak simultaneously, use backchannel cues like 'mhmm,' and delegate anything needing deep reasoning or search to a frontier model (GPT-5.5 at launch) mid-conversation. It ships in two sizes — GPT-Live-1 and GPT-Live-1 mini — as the new defaults for ChatGPT Voice, though video/screen-share voice and API access aren't available yet.

slides.title: GPT-Live Ends the Walkie-Talkie Era of Voice AI
slides.body: OpenAI's GPT-Live launched July 8, 2026 as a full-duplex voice model — it can listen and speak at the same time, instead of the strict turn-taking most voice assistants use today.
bullets: Announced July 8, 2026 on OpenAI's official blog, rolling out globally in ChatGPT Voice
Two sizes ship at launch: GPT-Live-1 (default for Go/Plus/Pro) and GPT-Live-1 mini (default for Free users)
Arrives just ahead of the broader GPT-5.6 rollout it's designed to sit in front of

slides.title: Turn-Taking Makes Voice Assistants Feel Scripted
slides.body: Most voice AI today is half-duplex: it waits for you to finish talking, processes, then responds, with no overlap.
bullets: That silence-then-answer rhythm is exactly what makes AI voice calls feel like a script instead of a conversation
Backchannel cues humans use to signal 'keep going' — a quick 'mhmm' or 'yeah' — are largely absent from half-duplex systems
Interrupting or being interrupted naturally isn't possible when the system can only listen OR speak at any given moment

slides.title: Full-Duplex: Listening and Speaking, Same Channel
slides.body: GPT-Live is built on a full-duplex architecture so it can listen and speak concurrently.
bullets: It can inject small backchannel responses like 'mhmm' or 'yeah' while the user is still talking, or stay quiet to give the user space to think
For anything needing web search, deeper reasoning, or heavier work, GPT-Live delegates to a frontier model behind the scenes and folds the result back into the live conversation
At launch, the frontier model behind that delegation is GPT-5.5

slides.title: Half-Duplex vs. Full-Duplex Voice AI

rows: Delegates live to a frontier model (GPT-5.5 at launch), returns result mid-conversation

slides.title: Thinking Out Loud Without Getting Cut Off
slides.body: Picture talking through a messy plan: 'I want to restructure my week... let me think... maybe move the gym to mornings...'
bullets: A half-duplex assistant either interrupts mid-thought or sits in dead silence waiting for a clear pause
GPT-Live can drop in a quiet 'mhmm' to signal it's listening, without breaking the user's train of thought
If the user then asks 'what's a good time to fit in a run before work,' GPT-Live delegates that reasoning to GPT-5.5 behind the scenes and speaks the answer back — without the conversation resetting

slides.title: What's Missing at Launch
slides.body: GPT-Live is a v1 release with real gaps.
bullets: Voice with video or screen sharing in ChatGPT isn't supported yet — OpenAI says it's coming, not available now
It's ChatGPT-only today; API access is planned but not yet open, so you can't build it into your own product
Free users get GPT-Live-1 mini, not the full model — expect a capability gap on anything requiring heavy delegated reasoning
Deciding when to backchannel vs. stay silent vs. interject is a judgment call the model can still get wrong — premature 'mhmm' can feel like an interruption too

slides.title: What to Remember About GPT-Live
bullets: Full-duplex = listen and speak on the same channel, enabling backchannel cues and natural interruption handling
GPT-Live-1 and GPT-Live-1 mini are the new defaults for ChatGPT Voice on paid and free tiers respectively
Heavy lifting (search, reasoning) is delegated live to a frontier model — GPT-5.5 at launch — rather than handled by the voice model itself
Video/screen-share voice and API access are on the roadmap, not available at launch

questions.prompt: What does 'full-duplex' mean in the context of GPT-Live?
options: It can listen and speak at the same time, rather than strictly taking turns
It supports two languages simultaneously
It runs on two GPUs for redundancy
It duplicates every response for accuracy
questions.explanation: Full-duplex is a communications term for simultaneous two-way flow — GPT-Live listens and speaks concurrently instead of the wait-then-respond pattern of prior voice assistants.

questions.prompt: When a GPT-Live conversation needs deep reasoning or a web search, what happens?
options: The conversation ends and restarts in text mode
GPT-Live delegates to a frontier model (GPT-5.5 at launch) behind the scenes and brings the result back into the live conversation
The user is asked to type the question instead
It always fails since GPT-Live can only make small talk
questions.explanation: GPT-Live handles the natural conversational layer itself but hands off anything requiring heavier reasoning or search to a frontier model, then folds the answer back into the ongoing voice conversation.

questions.prompt: Which capability is explicitly NOT available at GPT-Live's launch?
options: Backchannel cues like "mhmm"
A free-tier mini model
Voice with video or screen sharing in ChatGPT
Global rollout
questions.explanation: OpenAI stated that voice with video or screen sharing isn't supported at launch, with plans to add it later.

references.title: Introducing GPT-Live (OpenAI)

posts.title: LoRA: Fine-Tune a 70B Model by Training a Sliver of It
posts.subtitle: Freeze the pretrained weights, learn a low-rank correction, and merge it back in for zero added latency.
posts.summary: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) freezes a pretrained model's weights and injects small trainable low-rank matrices alongside them, cutting trainable parameters by orders of magnitude versus full fine-tuning. It merges back into the base weights at inference for zero added latency, and lets one frozen base model serve many task-specific adapters.

slides.title: LoRA: Fine-Tune a 70B Model by Training a Sliver of It
slides.body: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) freezes the pretrained weights and injects small trainable low-rank matrices next to them, rather than updating the whole model.
bullets: Introduced by Hu et al. at Microsoft Research in 2021; now the default way most teams fine-tune open LLMs
Cuts trainable parameters and optimizer memory by orders of magnitude versus full fine-tuning
The frozen base plus a small adapter can be merged back into a single matrix before serving

slides.title: Full Fine-Tuning Doesn't Scale Past One Model Per Task
slides.body: Updating every weight in a large model is expensive in both compute and storage.
bullets: Full fine-tuning updates every weight, so a 70B-parameter model needs gradients plus Adam's two extra moments per weight — commonly several times the base model's size in GPU memory
Every fine-tuned task produces an entirely new full-size checkpoint — 10 tasks means 10 full copies to store and swap between
Fine-tuning every weight end-to-end on a small task-specific dataset risks catastrophic forgetting of the base model's general capabilities

slides.title: Freeze the Big Matrix, Learn a Small Correction
slides.body: For a frozen weight matrix W, LoRA adds a learned update ΔW = B·A, where B and A are much smaller matrices whose inner dimension — the rank r — is typically 4 to 64.
bullets: Only A and B are trained; W never changes, so the optimizer only tracks gradients for the small low-rank pair instead of the full matrix
At inference, W + B·A can be merged into a single matrix — no added latency versus the original base model
One frozen base model can host many different LoRA adapters, swapped in per task or per customer without reloading the whole model
Combined with a quantized (e.g. 4-bit) frozen base, this becomes QLoRA — fine-tuning a large model on a single consumer GPU

slides.title: Full Fine-Tuning vs. LoRA vs. QLoRA

rows: Full model size (tens to hundreds of GB)
Same as LoRA, on a quantized base
Tens of MB — fits on a single consumer GPU

slides.title: One Base Model, Five Customer Tones
slides.body: A support-automation startup needs a 70B model to answer in five different tones: legal, medical, retail, technical, and casual.
bullets: With full fine-tuning: five separate full-size checkpoints, each needing its own storage and its own load into GPU memory to switch
With LoRA: one frozen base model stays resident in GPU memory, and each request loads a lightweight adapter (tens of MB) matching the right tone
Serving frameworks like S-LoRA can even batch requests across many different adapters against the same frozen base simultaneously

slides.title: Where LoRA Falls Short
slides.body: Rank and target-module choices matter, and LoRA isn't a universal replacement for full fine-tuning.
bullets: Too low a rank and the adapter can't capture the task shift (underfits); too high and you lose most of the efficiency win and risk overfitting a small dataset
LoRA typically targets specific weight matrices (commonly the attention Q/K/V/O projections) — skipping the right layers can leave a task under-adapted even at a high rank
Merging W + B·A for one adapter is trivial, but serving many adapters simultaneously without merging requires dedicated multi-adapter serving infrastructure
Not a substitute for full fine-tuning when the task needs genuinely new knowledge the base model never saw — LoRA adapts existing representations, it doesn't add large new capacity

slides.title: The Recipe in Four Lines
bullets: LoRA freezes pretrained weights and learns a low-rank correction ΔW = B·A, training a tiny fraction of parameters
One frozen base model can serve many task-specific adapters, and QLoRA extends this to fine-tuning on modest hardware
The adapter merges into the base weights at inference for zero added latency
Rank and target-module choice are the key hyperparameters — defaults aren't optimal for every task

questions.prompt: In LoRA, what happens to the original pretrained weight matrix W during fine-tuning?
options: It is frozen and never updated; only a small low-rank pair (A, B) is trained
It is fully retrained alongside the new adapter
It is deleted and replaced by A and B
It is quantized to 4-bit before training
questions.explanation: LoRA's core idea is freezing W entirely and learning a separate low-rank update B·A, which is what keeps the trainable parameter count so small.

questions.prompt: Why can a LoRA adapter be merged into the base model at inference with no added latency?
options: Because B·A has the same shape as W and can simply be summed into it before serving
Because the adapter runs on a separate GPU
Because inference always ignores the adapter
Because merging requires retraining the whole model first
questions.explanation: Since B·A has the same shape as W, it can simply be added into W ahead of time, producing one merged matrix — no extra matrix multiply is needed at serving time.

questions.prompt: What's a real risk of picking the rank r too high for a small fine-tuning dataset?
options: The model becomes faster
You lose most of LoRA's memory and parameter efficiency, and risk overfitting the small dataset
The base weights get corrupted
It's not possible to pick r too high
questions.explanation: A higher rank means more trainable parameters, approaching full fine-tuning's cost, and with a small dataset that extra capacity is more likely to memorize noise rather than generalize.

references.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models (arXiv)

posts.title: VideoChat3: One Open Model for Motion, Long Video, Streams
posts.subtitle: A single 4B-parameter architecture unifies fine-grained motion, hour-long reasoning, temporal grounding, and live streaming.
posts.summary: VideoChat3, the #1 trending paper on Hugging Face Daily Papers this week, is a fully open 4B-parameter video MLLM from Nanjing University's MCG-NJU group built to handle fine motion, hour-long video, temporal grounding, and live streaming with one architecture instead of separate specialist models. Two pieces make it work: I3D-ViT, which encodes motion directly in the vision tower, and Adaptive Frame Resolution, which spends visual tokens only where a frame actually needs closer inspection.

slides.title: A Single Open Model for Every Kind of Video Task
slides.body: Video understanding today is usually split across separate models: one tuned for short-clip motion, another for hour-long reasoning, another for pinpointing a moment in time, another for live streams. VideoChat3 (arXiv, July 2026) is built to do all four with one 4B-parameter checkpoint.
bullets: #1 trending paper on Hugging Face Daily Papers the week of July 17, 2026
Built by the Multimedia Computing Group at Nanjing University with collaborators including Limin Wang, Yu Qiao, Ziwei Liu, and Kai Chen
Fully open: weights, training code, and the data-construction pipeline are public on GitHub (MCG-NJU/VideoChat3)

slides.title: Video MLLMs Force a Choice: Fast, Long, or Precise
slides.body: A fixed frame-sampling strategy can't serve all three demands at once — that tension is why video understanding fragmented into specialist models in the first place.
bullets: Sampling every frame of an hour-long video at fixed resolution blows past any practical token budget — most of that footage is redundant background
Downsampling aggressively to fit the budget destroys the sub-second motion cues fine-grained action recognition depends on
Live streaming adds a third axis entirely: the model can't wait for the whole clip, it must process and answer as frames arrive

slides.title: I3D-ViT + Adaptive Frame Resolution
slides.body: Two architectural changes let one model cover the whole spectrum instead of trading one capability for another.
bullets: I3D-ViT (Inflated 3D Vision Transformer) inflates the patch embedding across a short temporal window, so motion is encoded in the vision tower itself, not inferred later from a sequence of static-image tokens
Adaptive Frame Resolution scores each frame's information content and spends a variable visual-token budget accordingly, instead of a fixed grid per frame
Together they cut the visual token count for high-frame-rate, long-form, and streaming inputs while preserving the spatiotemporal evidence needed to answer correctly

slides.title: LV116K: A Data Pipeline Built for Hour-Long Video
slides.body: A generalist model needs generalist training data — most video datasets are built from clips a few seconds to a few minutes long, which is part of why long-video reasoning has lagged behind long-context windows.
bullets: VideoChat3-LV116K is a dedicated data-construction pipeline targeting long-video-specific skills: tracking state across time, finding a needle-in-haystack moment, and re-identifying the same subject much later in the footage
The paper credits this pipeline, not just the architecture, for the model's gains specifically on long-video benchmarks

It's a reminder that architecture and data are usually a package deal — Adaptive Frame Resolution makes long video computationally feasible, LV116K is what teaches the model to actually reason over it

slides.title: Where the Gains Show Up
slides.body: The paper reports results across three task families rather than one leaderboard number — worth reading qualitatively since exact scores are in the full paper.

rows: Substantial gains, attributed to the LV116K data pipeline
Notable improvement locating the right moment in long footage
Strong performance maintained, not traded away for long-video gains

slides.title: Worked Example: Finding One Moment in an Hour of Footage
slides.body: Concrete scenario: a security team uploads a 1-hour warehouse camera feed and asks, "when did the forklift first enter aisle 7?"
bullets: A fixed-rate sampler must either sample sparsely (risking a missed entry frame) or densely (spending most of the token budget on ~59 minutes of empty aisle)
Adaptive Frame Resolution keeps token spend low on static stretches and raises it around motion or the queried object, so the model can localize the entry frame instead of guessing
Temporal-grounding training against benchmarks like ActivityNet is what lets it return a timestamp, not just a text description of "a forklift appears at some point"

slides.title: When a Generalist Video Model Isn't the Right Call
slides.body: Breadth across four task types is the point — but breadth has a cost.
bullets: At 4B parameters it's tuned for covering motion, long video, streaming, and grounding in one checkpoint, not for beating a specialist model narrowly fine-tuned on a single benchmark

Adaptive token allocation adds inference-time scoring overhead versus a fixed-grid sampler — a clear win on long or redundant video, closer to a wash on short clips where nearly every frame already matters
"Fully open" means weights, code, and data pipeline are public — it doesn't mean hour-long video is cheap to run; the visual token sequence for long footage still needs real GPU memory

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: VideoChat3 unifies four video tasks — fine motion, hour-long reasoning, temporal grounding, live streaming — that other MLLMs usually split across separate models, via two changes: I3D-ViT for motion and Adaptive Frame Resolution for token efficiency
A dedicated long-video data-construction pipeline (LV116K), not just a bigger context window, is credited for its long-video benchmark gains
It's the #1 trending paper on Hugging Face Daily Papers this week, from Nanjing University's MCG-NJU group, and ships fully open — weights, training code, and data pipeline
The tradeoff: generalist breadth at 4B parameters, with adaptive-token overhead that pays off most on long or redundant video and least on short clips

questions.prompt: What problem does Adaptive Frame Resolution solve?
options: It compresses the model's weights to fit on smaller GPUs
It varies the visual token budget per frame so redundant frames cost few tokens and information-dense frames get more, instead of a fixed grid for every frame
It replaces the vision transformer with a CNN
It only works on live streaming video, not recorded video
questions.explanation: Adaptive Frame Resolution scores each frame's information content and spends tokens accordingly — the fix for the "sample sparse and miss detail, or sample dense and blow the budget" tradeoff hour-long video creates.

questions.prompt: What does I3D-ViT change compared to feeding a language model a sequence of independently-encoded frame tokens?
options: It processes short frame clips jointly in the vision encoder so motion is captured before tokens reach the language model
It removes the vision transformer entirely and uses only text
It requires labeled bounding boxes for every frame
It only processes the first and last frame of a video
questions.explanation: I3D-ViT inflates the patch embedding across a temporal window, encoding motion directly in the vision tower rather than asking the language model to infer motion from a sequence of static-image tokens.

questions.prompt: What is VideoChat3-LV116K, and what is it credited for?
options: A quantization scheme that shrinks the model to run on mobile devices
A benchmark used only to measure live-streaming latency
A long-video data-construction pipeline credited with the model's gains on long-video benchmarks like Video-MME and LongVideoBench
An alternative name for the base 4B model with no architecture changes
questions.explanation: Short clips dominate most video training data, which is part of why long-video reasoning lags long-context windows. LV116K's pipeline specifically targets long-video skills like tracking state and re-identifying a subject much later.

references.title: VideoChat3: Fully Open Video MLLM for Efficient and Generalist Video Understanding
MCG-NJU/VideoChat3 (GitHub)

posts.title: Kimi K3: Moonshot's 2.8T Open Model Chases the Frontier
posts.subtitle: The largest open-weight model yet trades a hallucination spike for a frontend-coding lead — and triples its predecessor's price.
posts.summary: On July 16, 2026, Moonshot AI released Kimi K3 via API: a 2.8-trillion-parameter mixture-of-experts model (16-of-896 experts active) with a 1M-token context window, positioned against Claude Fable 5, Opus 4.8, and GPT-5.6 Sol. It tops LMArena's Frontend Code Arena but only lands mid-pack on general chat, and its accuracy gains come with a matching jump in hallucination rate. Full open weights are promised by July 27, 2026.

slides.title: The Largest Open-Weight Model Yet — Almost
slides.body: Moonshot AI (Beijing) released Kimi K3 via API on July 16, 2026: a 2.8-trillion-parameter mixture-of-experts model the company positions against Anthropic's Fable 5 and Claude Opus 4.8, and OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Sol.
bullets: 2.8T total parameters, but only 16 of 896 experts activate per token — roughly 50B active parameters, so inference cost tracks a much smaller model
Native multimodal input and a 1,048,576-token (1M) context window
Weights aren't out yet at launch — Moonshot committed to a full open-weight release by July 27, 2026

slides.title: Two Attention Tricks Behind the Efficiency Claim
slides.body: Moonshot attributes K3's reported ~2.5x scaling efficiency over its K2 generation to two architectural changes plus a new expert-routing framework.
bullets: Kimi Delta Attention (KDA): a hybrid linear-attention mechanism Moonshot reports enables markedly faster decoding than standard attention at long context

Attention Residuals (AttnRes): lets a layer selectively pull representations from earlier depths instead of accumulating every previous state, reported to improve training efficiency by roughly 25% over K2.6
Paired with a "Stable LatentMoE" routing framework for the 16-of-896 expert selection that keeps the huge total parameter count cheap to run

slides.title: Frontier Coding, Mid-Pack Chat, a Real Hallucination Cost
slides.body: Independent trackers (LMArena, Artificial Analysis) put K3 at the top of frontend-coding leaderboards but only mid-pack on general chat — and its jump in answer accuracy comes with a jump in confidently wrong answers.

rows: No leap over frontier chat rivals

slides.title: Worked Example: Is K3 Worth the Price Jump?
slides.body: A team paying K2.6's per-token rate for a coding agent has to decide whether K3's frontend-coding lead justifies switching.
bullets: K3 is priced at $3/million input tokens and $15/million output tokens (plus $0.30/million for cached input) — roughly triple K2.6's pricing, per independent trackers
For a coding-agent workload where the #1 Frontend Code Arena result is the deciding factor, the price jump can pay for itself in fewer retries and less human cleanup

For a general knowledge-QA workload, the hallucination-rate jump (~39%→~51%) means outputs need more verification, not less — the more accurate model is also the more confidently wrong one on that benchmark

slides.title: Read the Weights Release Date Carefully
slides.body: "Open-weight" claims at launch describe intent, not something you can download and audit on day one.
bullets: API-first, weights-later is now a common release pattern — don't assume "open-weight model" means the weights are available the day it's announced
Benchmark numbers this early come primarily from third-party leaderboards rather than Moonshot's own technical report — treat any specific score as provisional until that lands
The hallucination-rate increase is the clearest reason to pilot K3 on a narrow, verifiable task (like coding, where output can be tested) before trusting it on open-ended factual queries

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Kimi K3 is a 2.8T-parameter MoE model (16-of-896 experts active, ~50B active) from Moonshot AI, released via API on July 16, 2026, with full open weights promised for July 27
Two new mechanisms — Kimi Delta Attention (hybrid linear attention) and Attention Residuals — are Moonshot's explanation for a claimed ~2.5x scaling-efficiency gain over its K2 generation
It tops LMArena's Frontend Code Arena but sits mid-pack on general chat, and its accuracy gains on AA-Omniscience come with a matching jump in hallucination rate
At roughly 3x K2.6's price, the numbers argue for using it where output is checkable (coding) rather than where you'd have to trust it unverified

questions.prompt: Roughly how many of Kimi K3's 896 experts are active for any given token?
options: All 896
448 (half)
questions.explanation: K3 uses a sparse MoE routing scheme (16-of-896 experts, ~50B active parameters out of 2.8T total), which is why its inference cost tracks a much smaller dense model despite the huge total parameter count.

questions.prompt: What's the notable tradeoff in K3's benchmark results vs. its predecessor K2.6?
options: Reported accuracy rose, but its reported hallucination rate rose alongside it
Accuracy fell while hallucination also fell
Both accuracy and hallucination rate stayed flat
K3 wasn't evaluated on any comparable benchmark
questions.explanation: K3 answers more questions correctly on AA-Omniscience, but also states more wrong answers confidently — a real tradeoff, not an unambiguous upgrade, especially for workflows where trustworthiness matters more than raw accuracy.

questions.prompt: As of its July 16, 2026 launch, what's true about Kimi K3's "open-weight" status?
options: Full weights were downloadable immediately at launch
It's API-only with no plan to ever release weights
It launched via API first, with full open weights promised for July 27, 2026 — about 11 days later
It's closed-source and Moonshot has not commented on weights
questions.explanation: K3 shipped API-first on July 16; Moonshot committed to releasing the full open weights by July 27, 2026. Until that date, "open-weight" describes a promise, not something available to download and audit.

references.title: Kimi K3 2.8T-A50B: the largest open model ever released (AI News)
Kimi's open model K3 nears GPT-5.6 Sol and Fable 5

posts.title: GRPO: Reinforcement Learning Without a Critic Model
posts.subtitle: Skip the value network — score a group of sampled answers against each other and turn the ranking into a training signal.
posts.summary: GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization), introduced in DeepSeek's DeepSeekMath paper and later the backbone of DeepSeek-R1's reasoning training, drops PPO's learned critic model entirely. Instead, it samples a group of responses to the same prompt and normalizes each one's reward against the group's own mean and standard deviation — a model-free baseline that halves the memory footprint of RLHF-style training and has since spread into 2026's agentic-RL pipelines.

slides.title: Training LLMs to Reason Without a Second Model to Judge Them
slides.body: Classic RLHF (PPO) needs four models in memory at once: the policy, a frozen reference copy, a reward model, and a critic/value model that estimates how good a partial answer is. GRPO, introduced in DeepSeek's DeepSeekMath paper (Feb 2024), drops the critic entirely.
bullets: PPO's value/critic model is roughly the same size as the policy — for a 70B-parameter policy, that's another ~70B-parameter model just to estimate advantages
GRPO replaces it with a simple idea: sample a group of answers to the same prompt, and use their relative scores as the advantage signal
It's the RL algorithm behind DeepSeek-R1's reasoning training and is now widely reused across 2026's agentic-RL pipelines

slides.title: Why PPO Is Expensive for LLM Post-Training
slides.body: Standard PPO computes an advantage per token by subtracting a learned value estimate from the observed reward — and that value model is itself expensive and unstable to train.
bullets: Four models in memory during training: policy, frozen reference (for the KL penalty), reward model, and critic — each roughly policy-sized at LLM scale
The critic is trained on a moving target (the same policy it's judging), a well-known source of PPO instability
For math, coding, and reasoning tasks with a clear right answer, a learned per-token value estimate is solving a harder problem than the task actually needs

slides.title: The Group Is the Baseline
slides.body: For each prompt, GRPO samples a group of G responses (e.g. G=16) from the current policy, scores each with the reward function, and normalizes each response's reward against the group's own mean and standard deviation.
bullets: Advantage for response i = (reward_i − mean(group rewards)) / std(group rewards) — no learned value model required
Responses better than the group average get reinforced; responses below average get suppressed, all relative to what the current policy can already do on that exact prompt
A KL penalty against a frozen reference policy (same as PPO) keeps updates from drifting too far in a single step

slides.title: From Math Proofs to Agentic Tool Use
slides.body: GRPO's simplicity — no critic, one reward call per sampled response — is exactly what made it easy to reuse outside its original math-reasoning setting.
bullets: DeepSeekMath used it to push math reasoning without extra critic infrastructure; DeepSeek-R1 later used the same core algorithm to train chain-of-thought reasoning at scale
2026's agentic-RL work builds on GRPO's group-relative-advantage idea for multi-turn, tool-using agents, not just single-turn math answers

It composes well with verifiable rewards (unit tests pass/fail, a math answer checker) because the group-normalized signal doesn't need the reward to be smooth or dense — a binary pass/fail per sample is enough

slides.title: GRPO vs. PPO vs. DPO

rows: General RLHF, works off single rollouts
Reasoning RL with a verifiable or scorable reward (math, code)
No — no RL loop at all
No — trains on a fixed preference pair
Preference fine-tuning from an existing labeled/ranked dataset

slides.title: Worked Example: Training on a Single Math Prompt
slides.body: A simplified walk-through with a small group (real runs typically use larger groups, e.g. G=16 or more).
bullets: Prompt: "What is the derivative of x³ + 2x?" The policy samples 4 chain-of-thought answers; a rule-based checker scores each 1 (correct final answer) or 0 (incorrect)
Say the scores are [1, 1, 0, 0] → group mean = 0.5. The two correct answers get positive normalized advantage, the two incorrect ones get negative advantage
The policy is updated to make the correct-answer token sequences more likely and the incorrect ones less likely — purely from this one prompt's internal comparison, no separate value model needed

slides.title: Where GRPO Struggles
slides.body: Dropping the critic isn't free — the cost moves elsewhere.
bullets: Needs a reward that's cheap and reliable to compute many times per prompt (G samples × prompts per batch) — an expensive or noisy reward model erodes the benefit of dropping the critic

If every sampled response in a group gets the same score (all correct or all wrong), the group's standard deviation is near zero and the advantage signal collapses toward nothing for that prompt — wasted compute
Group sampling multiplies inference cost per training step (G rollouts instead of 1) even though it removes the critic — the savings are in memory and stability, not necessarily total compute

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: GRPO replaces PPO's learned critic with a model-free baseline: sample a group of responses to the same prompt and normalize each one's reward against the group's own mean and standard deviation

It cuts the memory footprint of RLHF-style training (policy + frozen reference, no separate critic) while working especially well with clear-cut, verifiable rewards like math correctness or passing tests
It's the algorithm behind DeepSeekMath and DeepSeek-R1's reasoning training, and its group-relative-advantage idea has since been extended into 2026's agentic and multi-turn RL work
The catch: it needs a cheap reward you can call G times per prompt, and groups where every sample scores identically waste the compute spent sampling them

questions.prompt: What does GRPO use instead of a learned critic/value model to compute the advantage for a sampled response?
options: A second, larger frozen model trained purely to imitate human raters
The response's reward normalized against the mean and standard deviation of a group of responses sampled for the same prompt
A hand-coded heuristic based on response length
Nothing — GRPO has no advantage signal at all
questions.explanation: GRPO samples a group of G responses per prompt, scores each with the reward function, and computes each one's advantage as (reward − group mean) / group std — no separate value network needed.

questions.prompt: Why does PPO typically need more memory than GRPO for the same policy size?
options: PPO requires 4x more GPUs for no algorithmic reason
PPO trains a separate critic/value model roughly the size of the policy itself, plus the reward model and frozen reference, while GRPO drops the critic
GRPO uses a smaller reward model than PPO
PPO doesn't support KL penalties
questions.explanation: PPO keeps a policy, frozen reference, reward model, and critic in memory — the critic alone is typically policy-sized. GRPO removes the critic and gets its baseline from the sampled group instead.

questions.prompt: What happens to GRPO's training signal for a prompt where every sampled response in the group gets the exact same reward?
options: The policy update becomes larger to compensate
Nothing changes, this case doesn't affect training
The group's standard deviation is near zero, so the normalized advantage collapses toward zero — that prompt contributes little to the update despite the sampling cost
GRPO automatically resamples with a larger group
questions.explanation: Advantage is reward normalized by the group's standard deviation. If every sample in the group scores identically (all correct or all wrong), there's no within-group signal to learn from — the compute spent sampling that group is largely wasted.

references.title: DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning in Open Language Models

posts.title: Quantization: Squeezing a 70B Model Onto One GPU
posts.subtitle: Round the weights to fewer bits, protect the ones that matter, and keep almost all the accuracy.
posts.summary: Post-training quantization stores each weight in fewer bits (16 to 8 to 4) to cut a model's memory footprint, but naive uniform rounding wrecks accuracy because real weight distributions have outliers. GPTQ fixes this with calibration-driven, sequential per-column correction; AWQ instead rescales the small fraction of activation-salient weight channels before rounding. Both can push a 70B model from ~140GB down to ~35GB with only a small accuracy cost — but push past 3 bits and quality typically falls off a cliff.

slides.title: A 70B Model Doesn't Fit on Your GPU
slides.body: Every parameter in a transformer is normally stored as a 16-bit float. That precision is nearly free during training, but at inference it's mostly wasted — and it's the single biggest cost of serving a large model.
bullets: A 70B-parameter model in fp16 needs roughly 140GB just for the weights, before activations or a KV cache
That's more memory than two 80GB GPUs, before serving a single token
Quantization asks: what if each weight were stored in 8, 4, or even fewer bits instead of 16?

slides.title: Naive Rounding Breaks the Model
slides.body: You can't just round every weight uniformly to a lower bit-width and call it done — real transformer weight distributions have long tails, and the largest values dominate the error.
bullets: Uniform (linear) quantization maps a weight's range onto a fixed number of buckets — 4 bits gives only 16 distinct levels for the entire range
A handful of outlier weights or activation channels stretch that range, so most 'normal' weights get crushed into just a few buckets and lose precision
Naive INT4 on an untouched model can cost several points of accuracy and send perplexity climbing — enough to make outputs noticeably worse

slides.title: GPTQ: Quantize Layer-by-Layer, Correct as You Go
slides.body: GPTQ (Frantar et al., 2022) treats quantization as an optimization problem, not a rounding rule.
bullets: It runs a small calibration dataset through the model and uses the resulting activation statistics to approximate each layer's Hessian — how sensitive the output is to each weight
Weights are quantized one column at a time; after each column, the remaining unquantized weights in that layer are nudged to compensate for the error just introduced
This sequential correction is what lets GPTQ push to 4 bits (sometimes 3) with only a small accuracy hit, without any retraining or gradient descent

slides.title: AWQ: Protect the ~1% of Weights That Actually Matter
slides.body: AWQ (Lin et al., 2023) skips the heavy Hessian math and asks a simpler question: which weight channels see the largest activations?
bullets: It observes activations on a small calibration set and finds the small fraction of weight channels — often around 1% — tied to consistently large activation magnitudes
Instead of leaving those channels at full precision, AWQ rescales them before quantizing, shrinking their rounding error without complicating the compute graph

No backpropagation and no per-layer reconstruction loop, so AWQ's calibration pass is markedly faster than GPTQ's — a practical reason it's a common default in serving stacks like vLLM and TensorRT-LLM

slides.title: Precision vs. Memory: The Direct Tradeoff

rows: Safe middle ground, minimal quality loss
Single high-memory GPU serving, near-fp16 quality

slides.title: Worked Example: Serving a 70B Chat Model on One GPU
slides.body: Concrete scenario: you want to serve a 70B-class chat model for a small team, and you have exactly one 48GB GPU.
bullets: At FP16 (~140GB) or even INT8 (~70GB), the model alone doesn't fit — you'd need multi-GPU tensor parallelism just to hold the weights
Quantize to 4-bit with AWQ or GPTQ: weights shrink to roughly 35GB, leaving headroom on a 48GB card for the KV cache and activations of several concurrent requests
The tradeoff you accept: slightly higher perplexity and a small per-multiply dequantization cost — usually a good trade for turning 'impossible on this hardware' into 'running today'

slides.title: When Quantization Bites Back
slides.body: Quantization is a real accuracy-for-memory trade, not a free win — and it doesn't fail evenly.
bullets: Below about 3 bits, quality typically falls off a cliff rather than degrading gracefully — most production deployments stop at 4 bits for exactly this reason
A calibration set that doesn't match real traffic (wrong language, wrong domain) can misidentify 'salient' channels, quietly hurting accuracy on exactly the inputs you care about
Quantizing weights alone doesn't reduce FLOPs — without kernels that natively compute in low precision, you save memory and bandwidth but not necessarily wall-clock compute
Some layers (often the first and last) are more sensitive than others; naively quantizing everything uniformly is why tooling like GGUF's K-quants supports mixed per-layer precision

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Quantization stores weights in fewer bits (16 to 8 to 4) to cut memory roughly in half at each step, making large models fit on smaller hardware
GPTQ uses calibration-derived Hessian approximations and sequential per-column correction to reach 4-bit with minimal accuracy loss, no retraining required
AWQ instead identifies and rescales the small fraction of activation-salient weight channels — faster to calibrate, no backprop, popular in serving frameworks
Below roughly 3 bits, accuracy drops sharply, and quantization saves memory/bandwidth more reliably than raw compute — pick your bit-width based on the hardware constraint you actually have

questions.prompt: Why can't you just uniformly round every weight in a transformer to 4 bits?
options: Transformers don't support integer math at all
Real weight distributions have outliers that stretch the range, crushing most 'normal' values into very few of the 16 available buckets
4-bit numbers can only represent even numbers
GPUs cannot store integers, only floats
questions.explanation: With 4 bits you only get 16 distinct levels to cover the whole range. Outlier weights stretch that range so most other weights collapse into just a few of those levels — which is why naive uniform rounding costs noticeable accuracy, and why methods like GPTQ and AWQ exist.

questions.prompt: What does GPTQ use to decide how to quantize each layer?
options: A fixed rounding rule applied identically to every model
Calibration-data-derived Hessian approximations, quantizing one column at a time and adjusting the remaining weights to compensate for the error just introduced
A second neural network trained to predict quantized weights
Random search over possible bit-widths
questions.explanation: GPTQ runs a calibration set through the model to approximate each layer's Hessian, then quantizes weights column-by-column, updating the not-yet-quantized weights after each step to correct for the rounding error — all without gradient descent or retraining.

questions.prompt: How does AWQ differ from GPTQ in what it protects?
options: AWQ quantizes every weight to a lower bit-width than GPTQ
AWQ identifies the small fraction of weight channels tied to large activation magnitudes and rescales them before quantizing, instead of computing a full Hessian-based correction
AWQ only works for 8-bit quantization, never 4-bit
AWQ requires full retraining of the model
questions.explanation: AWQ skips GPTQ's Hessian approximation and per-layer reconstruction loop. It instead finds the small share of activation-salient weight channels and rescales them to shrink their rounding error — a lighter calibration pass that's become a common default in serving stacks like vLLM.

references.title: GPTQ: Accurate Post-Training Quantization for Generative Pre-trained Transformers
AWQ: Activation-aware Weight Quantization for LLM Compression and Acceleration

posts.title: Multi-Agent Orchestration: When One Agent Isn't Enough
posts.subtitle: Supervisor, swarm, or debate — the pattern you pick decides whether more agents means more accuracy or just more cost
posts.summary: Multi-agent orchestration splits a task across several LLM agents instead of one — a supervisor delegating to specialists, a swarm working in parallel, or agents debating each other. Each pattern trades latency, cost, and coordination complexity differently, and the wrong pattern can multiply spend without multiplying quality.

slides.title: More Agents Isn't Automatically Better
slides.body: A single LLM call hits real limits on long, heterogeneous, or parallelizable tasks. Multi-agent orchestration splits the work across several agent instances — but the coordination pattern you choose matters as much as the split itself.
bullets: Used underneath deep-research tools, coding assistants with subagents, and enterprise workflow agents
The core question every pattern answers differently: who decides what each agent does, and who reconciles their outputs?

slides.title: Where a Single Agent Breaks Down
slides.body: One agent, one context window, one thread of reasoning — that model runs out of room well before real tasks do.
bullets: Context dilution: cramming a 10-step research task with 6 different tool schemas into one prompt degrades attention to any single part
No true parallelism: a single agent's tool calls are typically serialized inside one reasoning trace, so independent subtasks still run one after another
Role conflict: an agent that both drafts an answer and critiques it tends to be a lenient critic of its own work
Blast radius: one bad tool call or hallucinated fact can derail the entire remaining trajectory with nothing to catch it

slides.title: Three Core Coordination Patterns
slides.body: Almost every multi-agent system reduces to one of these shapes, or a mix.
bullets: Orchestrator-worker: a supervisor agent decomposes the task, dispatches subtasks to specialist workers, and synthesizes their results — the most common production pattern
Parallel swarm: identical or near-identical agents attack independent slices of the same problem simultaneously, then a merge step combines outputs
Debate / critic-actor: two or more agents argue different positions or one agent generates while another adversarially reviews, forcing errors to surface before the final answer ships

slides.title: The Orchestrator-Worker Loop in Practice
slides.body: The supervisor never does the work itself — it plans, delegates, and reconciles.
bullets: 1) Supervisor reads the task and breaks it into independent or sequential subtasks
2) Each subtask goes to a worker agent with only the context it needs — not the full conversation history
3) Workers run with their own tool access and return a bounded, structured result (not a raw transcript)
4) Supervisor reconciles results, resolves conflicts between workers, and produces the final output
Isolating each worker's context is the key win: a worker researching 'pricing' never sees the noise from the worker researching 'competitors'

slides.title: Worked Example: Comparing Three Vendors
slides.body: Task: 'Compare Vendor A, B, and C on pricing, SLAs, and integration effort, then recommend one.'
bullets: Supervisor spawns 3 worker agents in parallel, one per vendor, each with the same research tools but a scoped instruction
Each worker independently gathers pricing, SLA terms, and integration notes for just its vendor — no cross-contamination of facts
Workers return structured findings (not free-form essays), which the supervisor merges into one comparison
Supervisor makes the final recommendation, weighing the three structured results against the original ask
Wall-clock time: roughly one research round-trip, not three sequential ones — the reason to parallelize in the first place

slides.title: Pick the Pattern by What You're Optimizing For

rows: N x token cost, needs a merge step
Duplicate or contradictory outputs at merge
Critic can rubber-stamp instead of truly challenging

slides.title: Tradeoffs and Pitfalls
slides.body: Orchestration buys accuracy and parallelism, but never for free.
bullets: Cost multiplies, not adds: a 3-worker swarm is roughly 3x the token spend of one agent, before counting the supervisor's own calls
Coordination overhead is real work: writing subtask instructions, structured result schemas, and conflict resolution logic is often harder than the task itself
Errors can compound across agents just as easily as within one — a worker that hallucinates a fact can poison the supervisor's synthesis if nothing checks it
Skip orchestration when: the task is short, has no independent subtasks, or a single well-scoped agent already solves it reliably — added agents just add latency and cost with no accuracy gain

slides.title: Match the Shape of the Task to the Shape of the System
slides.body: The orchestration pattern should mirror how the task actually decomposes — not be bolted on because 'agents' sounds more capable.
bullets: Sequential, dependent steps → orchestrator-worker with a supervisor that passes results forward
Independent, similarly-shaped subtasks → parallel swarm for wall-clock speed
High-stakes or ambiguous correctness → debate/critic pattern to force adversarial review
Always isolate each worker's context to just what it needs — shared full-history context is the fastest way to blow the cost budget
Default to a single agent first; add orchestration only when a concrete bottleneck (context, parallelism, or role conflict) demands it

questions.prompt: In the orchestrator-worker pattern, why does each worker typically get only a scoped slice of context instead of the full conversation history?
options: Workers are not allowed to use tools
Isolating context avoids diluting the worker's attention with irrelevant history and prevents cross-contamination between subtasks
It's a hard technical limit of all LLM APIs
The supervisor cannot generate more than one prompt
questions.explanation: A worker researching one vendor doesn't need the noise from another vendor's research thread. Scoped context keeps each worker focused and keeps token spend down, which is the main practical reason to isolate context per worker.

questions.prompt: A parallel swarm of 4 agents working the same task roughly costs how much compared to a single agent, before the merge step?
options: The same, since LLM calls are billed per task not per agent
Roughly 4x the token cost, since each agent runs its own full reasoning pass
Always cheaper, because parallel calls share a context window
Exactly half, due to caching
questions.explanation: Cost multiplies with agent count in a swarm — 4 independent agents each running their own reasoning and tool calls is roughly 4x the token spend of one agent, which is the central tradeoff against the wall-clock speedup.

questions.prompt: When should you skip multi-agent orchestration and use a single agent instead?
options: Whenever the task involves any tool calls at all
When the task is short, has no independent subtasks, and a single well-scoped agent already handles it reliably
Never — multi-agent systems always outperform single agents
Only when working with open-source models
questions.explanation: Orchestration adds real coordination overhead, latency, and multiplied cost. If a task doesn't decompose into independent or clearly separable subtasks, adding agents just adds expense without an accuracy or speed payoff.

posts.title: Agents' Last Exam: Where Frontier Agents Score 0%
posts.subtitle: Berkeley's new benchmark trades toy tasks for 1,500+ real jobs — and top agents still can't clear the hardest tier.
posts.summary: Agents' Last Exam (ALE) is a Berkeley RDI benchmark built from 1,500+ real professional tasks across 55 industries. Frontier agents average 26% overall but collapse to near 0% on the hardest tier — even models that score 82% on Terminal-Bench.

slides.title: Built to Break Benchmark Saturation
bullets: Berkeley RDI + 300 industry experts, published June 2026 (arXiv 2606.05405)
1,500+ tasks across 55 sub-industries, drawn from real professional work
Frontier agents: ~26% overall, but near 0% on the hardest tier

slides.title: Existing Benchmarks Were Topping Out
slides.body: Models were clearing 80%+ on benchmarks like Terminal-Bench, but that stopped tracking whether an agent could actually do a client's job.
bullets: Codex with GPT-5.5 scores 82% on Terminal-Bench — a benchmark widely treated as 'solved'
ALE asks a harder question: does that translate into real, deliverable work?
300+ professionals (Berkeley, MIT, Morgan Stanley, Adobe, AstraZeneca) contributed tasks that took them days or weeks to complete originally

slides.title: Real Sandboxes, Deterministic Grading
bullets: Agents get one task description and an unconstrained real OS sandbox — no scaffolding hints
Final artifacts are graded by deterministic, reproducible code evaluators, not human judges
Tasks span 55 sub-industries in 13 clusters, mapped to the U.S. O*NET/SOC occupational taxonomy
Three difficulty tiers: near-term, full-spectrum, and last-exam

slides.title: Solved Benchmark vs. Real Task
slides.body: Same model, wildly different scores depending on whether the task looks like a benchmark or looks like a job.

slides.title: Tasks Look Like Client Work, Not Trivia
slides.body: There's no multiple-choice version of ALE — every task is a real deliverable pulled from an actual job.
bullets: Contributors uploaded real past projects: think a finance team's client-ready model, a creative agency's production asset, a lab's protocol review
An agent has to plan, execute, and produce the final artifact end-to-end inside the sandbox — not just answer a question about it
Grading checks the artifact itself against deterministic criteria, the same way a human reviewer would check deliverables

slides.title: What ALE Doesn't Tell You
bullets: It's a snapshot: VentureBeat reported GPT-5.5 already overtaking Claude Fable 5 head-to-head within weeks of release
Deterministic code grading is reproducible, but can penalize partially-correct or creatively-valid solutions a human might accept
The public corpus is 1,500 of a planned 5,000 tasks — today's ~26% baseline will keep moving as coverage grows

slides.title: Benchmark-Topping Isn't Job-Ready
bullets: An agent that scores 82% on Terminal-Bench can still fail 100% of the hardest real tasks in the same domain
The gap is in sustained, long-horizon execution — not single-step correctness
If you're deciding whether to trust an agent with real economic work, track ALE-style evals, not just leaderboard scores

questions.prompt: What does ALE primarily grade agents against?
options: Multiple-choice trivia questions
Real, economically valuable professional tasks with deterministic evaluators
Chatbot conversation quality
Pure math olympiad problems
questions.explanation: ALE draws tasks from 55 sub-industries mapped to real professional work, and grades the final artifact with deterministic code evaluators rather than human judges or multiple-choice format.

questions.prompt: On ALE's hardest 'last-exam' tier, how did frontier agents — including ones that score 82% on Terminal-Bench — perform?
options: Around 80%
Around 50%
Near 0%, averaging roughly 2.6%
A perfect 100%
questions.explanation: Despite topping existing benchmarks, frontier agents averaged only about 2.6% on ALE's hardest tier, with many configurations scoring a flat 0% — showing benchmark performance doesn't guarantee real-world task completion.

questions.prompt: Why might a model that dominates Terminal-Bench still collapse on ALE?
options: ALE uses easier tasks than Terminal-Bench
ALE only tests short, single-turn prompts
ALE demands sustained, long-horizon execution on unconstrained real-world tasks, which surfaces failure modes shorter benchmarks hide
